Table of Contents
  • Act 1 at a Glance
    • The Nine Beats
  • Threads Running Through Act 1
    • Dreyfus: The Dog That Never Leaves
    • Grafvitnir: The Bound Enemy (GM Secret)
    • Gragnir: The Ash-Walker’s Arc
    • Campaign Currencies in Act 1
  • Level Progression
  • Act 1 Encounter Budget
    • Combat Encounters
    • Montage Tests
    • Negotiation Encounters
  • Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Act 1 Scale)
  • What Act 1 Establishes

The heroes are summoned, forged together by hardship, and sent on a desperate quest to find the one person who might save Svellheim from eternal winter.

Act 1 at a Glance

Levels: 1 → 3
Sessions: 10–16 (adjust pacing to table)
Tone: Survival, paranoia, desperate hope, road-fellowship
Arc: Strangers become companions; the crisis is confirmed as existential

The Nine Beats

Beat Name Sessions Summary
1 The Konungs-ferð ½ The summons reaches the PCs' homes. They are chosen—or volunteered.
2 The Road to Hrafnborg ½–1 Travel montage. PCs meet on the road. Draugr ambush at a graveyard ruin.
3 The Quiet Summons 1 Polite custody in Hrafnborg. Harald reveals the crisis and the mission: find Kaelen. Gragnir names the first step: find Lew. Dreyfus (the hound) listens.
4 The Road to Stairwatch 1–2 Sneaking out of Hrafnborg. 200 miles / ~10 days. Two Hard montage tests, combat (Frost-Draugr), negotiation (Chainwarden Toll), POIs.
5 Finding Lew 1–2 Stairwatch. Negotiation with Lew. Local tasks. The false eye is revealed. Lew agrees to guide them.
6 The Great Green 2–3 160 miles (Ashen Woods) or 125 miles (Bone Fields). Route choice. Three Hard montage rounds, 2 combats, POIs, Grafvitnir network pressure.
7 The Reluctant Hearth 1–2 Kaelen’s hidden grove. Very Hard negotiation. She comes—with conditions or after an attack forces her hand.
8 The Long Road Home 2–3 220 miles / ~12 days. Three Moderate montage rounds, negotiation (Greyfen Holdfast), Gragnir reveals Dreyfus’s master.
9 The Siege of Hrafnborg 1–2 Hrafnborg is besieged by undead. Montage test to breach the siege. The Ever-Embers are failing because Yggdrasil is dying. The party must journey to the Emberwell. Act 1 ends; Act 2 begins.

Threads Running Through Act 1

Dreyfus: The Dog That Never Leaves

Dreyfus appears first as High King Harald’s grey hound in Beat 3. He is present for every key conversation in Hrafnborg. He does not eat. He does not sleep. He watches mouths more than hands.

Dreyfus never leaves the King’s side. Throughout Act 1—and all of Act 2—he remains in dog form beside Harald, listening to every war council, every messenger’s report, every whispered conversation in the King’s chambers. He relays intelligence to Grafvitnir through Wyrd-channels: root-whispers that travel through Yggdrasil’s dying network, received by the Grave-Wolf’s other servants and passed to Pale Maw operatives in the field.

The party never sees Dreyfus on the road. The interference they encounter comes from Grafvitnir’s network—Pale Maw agents, corrupted officials, informant chains—all acting on intelligence that Dreyfus provides from Hrafnborg.

Beat 3: Dreyfus hears the mission.
Beats 4–7: The Grafvitnir Pressure Check activates. Pale Maw agents, corrupted informants, and Wyrd-touched creatures create complications—acting on intelligence relayed from Hrafnborg. The PCs don’t know the source.
Beat 8: Gragnir reveals the truth: Dreyfus is not a dog. He is a shape-walker, an agent of Grafvitnir—and he has been sitting beside Harald this entire time. The party cannot confront him from over two hundred miles away.
Beat 9: The party returns to Hrafnborg knowing the King’s hound is the enemy’s spy. Dreyfus is still there, still watching. This tension carries into Act 2.
End of Act 2: Dreyfus finally reveals himself and attacks Harald directly.

Grafvitnir: The Bound Enemy (GM Secret)

⚠ Director Secret: The Grave-Wolf

Grafvitnir is the campaign’s true antagonist—not Níðhöggr (which is entropy, mindless gnawing), but a being of intelligent malice imprisoned in the deepest root-chamber of Yggdrasil.

Grafvitnir was bound during the First Kindling, when the original Ember-Keepers sealed the root-chamber and lit the Ever-Embers as both warmth-anchors and prison-locks. As Fimbulwinter weakens Yggdrasil, Grafvitnir’s prison thins. Every Ever-Ember that dies is a lock that opens.

Dreyfus is Grafvitnir’s shape-walker—a fragment of will given form and sent into the world to watch, listen, and hasten the tree’s death. He remains at Harald’s side because that is where the most valuable intelligence flows: troop movements, supply routes, diplomatic messages, and the PCs' mission details. He relays this through Wyrd-channels—root-whispers carried along Yggdrasil’s dying network to Grafvitnir’s other agents.

Dreyfus cannot directly harm those under guest-right (he is still Wyrd-bound by the rules of the world he wants to destroy). He can only observe and report. Grafvitnir’s other servants—Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures—act on the intelligence he provides.

This is not revealed until Beat 8. Until then, the PCs should attribute complications to the Pale Maw, bandits, or bad luck. When they learn the truth, the horror is that the spy is back in Hrafnborg with the King and they can do nothing about it from the road. This tension drives the return journey and carries into Act 2, culminating when Dreyfus finally reveals himself and attacks Harald.

Gragnir: The Ash-Walker’s Arc

Gragnir is the campaign’s Gandalf/Allanon figure—he provides direction, not answers.

Beat 3: Appears in Harald’s sitting room. Names Lew as the key to finding Kaelen.
Beat 5: Gragnir and Lew share a history (the false eye is Gragnir’s gift). This emerges through Lew’s stories, not exposition.
Beats 6–7: Gragnir may appear at a critical moment—a fork in the road, a dire combat, a moral crossroads—but he does not stay.
Beat 8: Gragnir meets the full party on the return road and delivers the truth about Dreyfus and Grafvitnir. This is the campaign’s first major lore reveal. The horror: the spy is back in Hrafnborg and they can do nothing from over two hundred miles away.

Campaign Currencies in Act 1

Heat (fuel, food, warmth): the dominant scarcity. Every travel montage costs Heat. Losing Heat means cold, exhaustion, and death.
Weight (tools, weapons, transport): secondary. Equipment damage on failed montages, repair costs in settlements, trade goods for negotiation.
Word (reputation, oaths, legal standing): social currency. Forged papers, faction favors, Chainwarden warrants, and the Konungs-ferð oath itself are all measured in Word.

Level Progression

Beat Milestone Level
1–2 Survive the road and the draugr ambush 1
3–4 Accept the mission and reach Stairwatch 2
5–7 Find Lew and Kaelen; the party is forged 3
8–9 Return to besieged Hrafnborg; the truth revealed 3 (entering Act 2 at 4)

Act 1 Encounter Budget

Combat Encounters

Beat Encounter Difficulty Notes
2 Draugr ambush at the graveyard ruin Moderate First real fight. Teach the system.
4 Frost-Draugr on the Mountain Road (2 Frost-Draugr + 3 Grave-Wisps) Moderate Between the two montage tests. Full stat blocks in beat file.
5 Stairwatch complications (bar fight, thugs, Chainwarden confrontation) Easy–Moderate Social with teeth. Can be avoided.
6 Rot-Beasts at the Treeline (2 Rot-Beasts + 1 Vine Strangler) Moderate Ashen Woods route. Terrain matters—charcoal trunks, ash drifts, hidden roots.
6 Barrow-Draugr Ambush (3 Barrow-Draugr + 1 Grave-Singer) Moderate Bone Fields alternate route. Substitutes for Rot-Beasts if PCs take this path.
6 Rootgnaw Brood (4 Grubs + 1 Queen Boss L3) Hard Near the Green Heart boundary. Both routes converge here. Living roots as terrain.
7 Green Heart guardians (dryads, Rot-warped landvaettir) Hard Triggered on negotiation failure OR as separate encounter.
8 Return road encounters (draugr patrols, blizzard hazards) Moderate–Hard Escalation. The dead are organized and converging on Hrafnborg.
9 Siege breakthrough Hard Climactic Act 1 combat. Getting through the undead to Hrafnborg’s walls.

Montage Tests

Beat Montage Difficulty
2 The road to Hrafnborg Easy
4 Hrafnborg to Stairwatch — TWO separate Hard montages (200mi, ~10 days) Hard
6 Stairwatch to Green Heart — 3 rounds (160mi Ashen Woods / 125mi Bone Fields) Hard
8 Green Heart to Hrafnborg — 3 rounds (220mi, ~12 days, worsening weather) Moderate
9 Breaching the siege of Hrafnborg Hard

Negotiation Encounters

Beat Negotiation Difficulty
4 Chainwarden Toll at Greypass (Warden-Sergeant Eirik) Moderate (Interest 5, Patience 4)
5 Bargaining with Lew Moderate (Interest 6, Patience 5)
7 Convincing Kaelen to return Very Hard (Interest 5, Patience 4, with pitfalls)
8 Greyfen Holdfast (Thyra Blackhand, refugee camp) Moderate (Interest 5, Patience 4)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check (Act 1 Scale)

After each travel montage or major faction scene (starting in Beat 4), make a secret d6 check. This represents Grafvitnir’s network acting on intelligence Dreyfus relays from Hrafnborg—not Dreyfus himself, who never leaves the King’s side.

Roll Result
1–3 The network does not act this beat. Intelligence is still flowing, but Grafvitnir’s agents haven’t caught up or aren’t in position.
4–5 Network interference: Pale Maw agents spoil rations, tip off a Chainwarden patrol, plant a rumor that poisons a negotiation, or a stranger on the road asks too many questions about where the PCs are going.
6 Major intervention: Pale Maw operatives attempt a direct interception—an ambush, a planted agent posing as a traveller, or intelligence passed to local authorities that puts the PCs' cover at risk.

Escalation: Starting in Beat 6, change the scale: 1–2 = no action, 3–5 = network interference, 6 = major intervention.

Starting in Beat 8, change again: 1 = no action, 2–4 = network interference, 5–6 = major intervention.

What Act 1 Establishes

By the end of Act 1, the players should know:

  • Svellheim is dying. The Ever-Embers are failing because Yggdrasil itself is dying.

  • An ancient evil—Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf—is imprisoned in Yggdrasil’s roots and its prison is weakening as the tree withers.

  • Dreyfus was never a dog. He is Grafvitnir’s agent—and he is still at Harald’s side, listening to every word. The King doesn’t know.

  • The party must journey to the Emberwell and renew the spark of life in Yggdrasil. This is their Act 2 quest.

  • Kaelen carries the knowledge of the old rites. Lew can find the way. The party provides the will.

  • Every faction has its own agenda, and the heroes cannot trust institutions—only each other.

Design Goals

  • Replace external-setting cosmology with Svellheim’s: Yggdrasil as infrastructure, Wyrd as Word made inevitable (oath-infrastructure), the Rot as infrastructure failure.
  • Keep pressure character-driven (Grafvitnir's network, fed by Dreyfus at the king's side), not clock-driven.
  • Keep every phase playable with Draw Steel’s strengths: tactical combat, Negotiations, Montage Tests, tiered consequences.
  • Preserve the campaign’s central moral blade: the world can be saved, but not for free.

The Belts and Choke-Points (Campaign Region)

Svellheim is divided into three survivability zones ("Belts"). Restore the Flame is set three years into Fimbulwinter, with play focused on the Coast and the perilous routes inland.

Use borders as logistics, not map lines: who controls the flow of Heat, Weight, and Word.

  • Coastal Belt: The Splintered Fjords (Hrafnborg)
    • Refugee city where rationing is law.
    • Power center for the Chainwardens (Word for profit; tariffs and contracts).
  • Interior Belt: the routes that still (sometimes) work
    • The Lantern Road (Word + Weight): wayfinders, news, and transport.
    • The Red-Rivet Compact (Weight): smiths, tools, iron as survival.
    • The Ashwright Kins (Heat): peat, oil, coal, and warmth monopolies.
    • The Quiet Wardens (Word): burial rites, Barrow-Law, and keeping the dead down.
  • Pressure from other powers
    • The Blackwater Vow (Word + Weight): martial law, raids, consolidation by force.
    • The Loom-Circle (Word): seidr, healing, and bargains that feel like fate.
    • The Pale Maw (Void): doomsday cult sabotage—"mercy" through ending.

The Emberwell (Campaign Keystone)

The Emberwell is not a public location on a map. It is a deep infrastructure-knot where Yggdrasil’s circulation still almost holds.

Use existing geography:

  • The Throat of the World is the visible landmark (fire-and-ice country).
  • The Steam-Oasis is the survivable shell and staging ground.
  • The Emberwell is the sealed chamber below—half geothermal engine, half root-knot.

What people believe:

  • The Emberwell is "where warmth comes from," or "where the world’s last hearth still breathes."
  • The Need-Fire is an old rite that might help.

What is true (GM):

  • The Emberwell is a failing junction in Yggdrasil’s system.
  • The system cannot be restarted without a price paid in Word made flesh.

Optional (GM): the Ember-Keepers (a secret society) chase the same goal—reigniting the Sun—and their experiments with Soul-Fuel are either a rival solution or a terrible shortcut.

The Unseen Enemy (Name Spoken in Hushes)

The name people use: The Rootgnaw

Everyone has a different story. Nobody has proof.

  • Some say it is a worm in the world’s bones.
  • Some say it is a wolf chewing the sun’s leash.
  • Some say it is the dead scratching from below.

The Rootgnaw is an ephemeral enemy: never seen, never directly fought.

The truth (GM): Níðhöggr

In your cosmology, Níðhöggr gnaws the roots and anchor points of reality.

In play, heroes fight:

  • the Rot’s manifestations,
  • the Rootgnaw’s brood (wights, beasts, landvaettir gone wrong),
  • and the humans who weaponize collapse.

Campaign Antagonists

The Pale Maw (Faction)

The Pale Maw are a doomsday cult whose mercy is annihilation.

They are not cosmic gods. They are people who have decided:

  • law is a lie,
  • warmth is cruelty rationed,
  • and silence is peace.

They sabotage Heat, Weight, and Word to speed the end.

Dreyfus (The Hound)

Dreyfus is a shape-walker bound to Grafvitnir. He never leaves the King’s side—he IS the King’s grey hound, lying by the fire, listening to every whispered plan. He is the campaign’s intelligence source, not its field agent:

  • he overhears the party’s plans in Hrafnborg,
  • he relays intelligence via Wyrd-channels (root-whispers through Yggdrasil’s dying network),
  • Grafvitnir’s other agents (Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures) act on that intelligence in the field.

Table-facing introduction: Dreyfus appears only as the King’s hound in the room—quiet, listening, and present for secrets. He never transforms or leaves while the party is in Hrafnborg.

The network does the work: Grafvitnir’s agents use warrants, forged seals, bribed Chainwardens, and Wyrd-touched carrion birds (silver-eyed Valravn) to pressure the party on the road. Dreyfus never leaves Hrafnborg until the end of Act 2, when he reveals himself and attacks Harald.

Key NPCs

High King Harald

Role: Desperate monarch, tactical pragmatist, oathbound ruler

Appearance: Greying, ink-stained hands, wearing simple clothes (no crown in private). Looks older than his legend suggests.

Demeanor: Blunt, exhausted, but still commanding.

Motivation: Save Hrafnborg. Save Svellheim. Protect his people at any cost—even if that cost is his own legitimacy.

High King Harald — Draw Steel NPC

Level 7 Brute Leader · EV 28 · Humanoid, Hearthborn, King

Stamina: 160Speed: 6 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 2Free Strike: 7

Characteristics: Might +5 · Agility +2 · Reason +2 · Intuition +3 · Presence +4

Features
  • End Effect: Harald can take 5 damage to immediately end one “save ends” effect on himself.
  • Supernatural Insight: Harald ignores supernatural concealment.
Signature Abilities
  • Hit and Run (Action, Melee, Might): Staying in constant motion helps you slip out of reach after a brutal assault. Deals 2/5/7 + Might damage; shift 1 after the attack. Tier 3: target is slowed (save ends).
  • Parry (Triggered, Signature): When hit, Harald shifts 1 square and takes half damage; reduces potency by 1.
Heroic Abilities
  • Your Entrails Are Your Extrails! (3 Rage, Melee, Might): 3/5/8 + Might damage; target is bleeding (save ends). While bleeding, the target takes 5 (Might) damage at the end of each of Harald’s turns.
  • En Garde! (5 Rage, Melee, Agility): High-damage dueling strike; triggers an exchange of free strikes with the target.

Negotiation: Interest 7 · Patience 4

Director Notes: Run Harald as a war-king: decisive, practical, and unsentimental. In battle, he is the hearth made iron—an old fury disciplined into law. In Phase 1, Harald is not fighting—he’s confessing. If Harald enters combat, the situation has catastrophically collapsed.

Key Trait: Harald does not lie. He omits, he redirects, but he does not lie.

Gragnir, the Ash-Walker

Role: Wandering seidr-worker, fragment-keeper, cryptic guide (Allanon-role)

Appearance: Tall, gaunt, ash-stained hands, smoke-grey furs, smells of burning peat.

Demeanor: Sardonic, blunt. Never lies, but never tells the whole truth.

Motivation: Keep the world’s infrastructure from total collapse—but through the PCs’ hands, not his own.

Gragnir, Ash-Walker — Draw Steel NPC

Level 10 Controller Elite · EV 48 · Humanoid, Druid

Stamina: 220Speed: 7 (walk, climb)Size: M
Free Strike: 10

Characteristics: Might +0 · Agility +2 · Reason +4 · Intuition +5 · Presence +3

Features
  • End Effect: Gragnir can take 5 damage to immediately end one “save ends” effect on himself.
  • Supernatural Insight: Gragnir ignores supernatural concealment.
Signature Ability
  • The Green Within, the Green Without (Action, Ranged 10, Reason): Whipping vines erupt from a foe’s body to grasp at another close by. Deals 2/5/7 + Reason damage; slide one creature within 10 squares of the target up to 2 squares.
Heroic Abilities
  • Thorn Cage (11 Essence, Ranged 10, Intuition): 10/15/21 + Intuition damage; target is restrained (save ends). Ongoing 10 damage.
  • The Wode Remembers and Returns (9 Essence, Burst 4): Creates a persistent zone of difficult terrain; allies gain cover and begin recovering in the zone.
  • Heart of the Wode (11 Essence): Summons a size-5 tree. Allies adjacent can Catch Breath as a free maneuver; enemies adjacent are restrained.
  • The Green Defends Its Servants (11 Essence, Maneuver): Grants 30 temporary stamina; the shield explodes when depleted, dealing 10 damage.
  • O Flower Aid, O Earth Defend (5 Essence, Maneuver, Cube 3 within 10): Creates a persistent zone that heals allies, creates difficult terrain, and damages enemies.
  • So Gullible (5 Essence, Free Triggered): When a creature targets Gragnir, he teleports away and makes a free strike against the attacker.
  • Word of Guidance (Triggered): When an ally within 10 squares makes an ability roll, Gragnir grants them an edge.

Free Strike: Hurl Element (Ranged 10, Reason): Hurls elemental energy, choosing the damage type.

Negotiation: Interest 5 · Patience 5

Director Notes: Run Gragnir as a mentor who is protective but withholding. In combat, he is a devastating controller—Thorn Cage immobilizes priority targets, Heart of the Wode reshapes the battlefield, and So Gullible punishes anyone foolish enough to target him directly.

Key Trait: Gragnir will not do the work for the PCs. He points, he warns, he provides tools—but he will not save them.

Kaelen (The Seventh)

Role: Campaign emotional core, broken Ember-Keeper, reluctant guide

Appearance: Early twenties, ember-mark scar on forehead (faded but visible), haunted eyes, practical traveler’s gear.

Demeanor: Guarded, guilt-ridden, fiercely protective of common folk.

Backstory: Kaelen was the youngest Ember-Keeper ever chosen (age 13). She served faithfully for a year, then abandoned the order without explanation. The truth (GM secret, revealed in Phase 2): she broke guest-right, and the first draugr rose because of it.

Kaelen (The Seventh) — Draw Steel NPC

Level 5 Support Leader · EV 20 · Humanoid, Censor

Stamina: 80Speed: 5 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 1Free Strike: 5

Characteristics: Might +3 · Agility +1 · Reason +1 · Intuition +2 · Presence +4

Signature Abilities
  • Judgement (Maneuver, Ranged 10): You utter a prayer that outlines your foe in holy energy. The target is judged until end of encounter. Whenever the judged target uses a main action within Kaelen’s line of effect, she deals 2×Presence holy damage as a free triggered action. Spend 1 wrath to additionally: prevent shifts + melee free strike, impose a bane on the target’s power roll, reduce potency by 1, or taunt the target.
  • Protective Attack (Action, Melee, Might): Melee strike that taunts the target, drawing their attention away from allies.
Heroic Abilities
  • Righteous Judgment (9 Wrath, Melee): High-damage strike. When Kaelen hits a judged target, all allies who can see it can surge.
  • Edict of Stillness (7 Wrath, Maneuver): Creates an aura 2 zone around Kaelen. Enemies who leave the zone take holy damage.
  • Blessing of Steel (11 Wrath, Maneuver): Creates an aura 5 zone. Enemies take a bane on attacks; allies gain damage immunity 5.
  • Win This Day! (9 Wrath, Burst 3): All allies in burst surge 2, spend a recovery, and remove one “save ends” condition.

Triggered: Parry — When hit, Kaelen shifts 1 square and takes half damage; reduces potency.

Negotiation: Interest 5 · Patience 4
Motivations: Redemption, Duty, Protecting common folk
Pitfalls: Glory, Orders from authority, Minimizing her guilt

Director Notes: Play Kaelen as gruff, cynical, and duty-bound. She helps because she must, not because she believes she deserves redemption. In combat, Kaelen is a force multiplier—Judgement punishes a single priority target relentlessly while Win This Day! can swing a losing fight. Her Negotiation stats matter: convincing Kaelen to return is a major challenge in Phase 2. Appeal to duty and redemption; avoid invoking authority or glory.

Key Trait: Kaelen’s guilt is Svellheim-native. She broke an oath, and reality broke with it.

Llewyellen “Lew” Tosferd (The Finder)

Role: Practical miracle-worker, information broker, cost-accountant

Appearance: Middle-aged, weathered, always smiling (even when the smile doesn’t reach his eyes).

Demeanor: Friendly, transactional, never offers anything for free.

Function: Lew can find things that are lost—including people. He uses a mix of old contracts, informant networks, and (maybe) subtle magic he won’t admit to.

Llewyellen “Lew” Tosferd — Draw Steel NPC

Level 4 Support Standard · EV 16 · Humanoid, Shadow

Stamina: 60Speed: 6 (walk)Size: M
Stability: 0Free Strike: 4

Characteristics: Might +0 · Agility +3 · Reason +2 · Intuition +4 · Presence +3

Features
  • Crafty: Lew doesn’t provoke opportunity attacks by moving.
  • Keep It Down (Shadow 4th-level): While conversing with someone who shares his language, Lew decides whether anyone else can perceive what he’s conveying—even while yelling or in a crowded room.
  • Smoke Bomb (Shadow / Caustic Alchemy): Lew can use the Hide maneuver even while observed and without cover or concealment. He shifts 3 squares (Agility), and if he ends with cover or concealment, he is automatically hidden.
Signature Abilities
  • I’m No Threat (Maneuver, Magic / Harlequin Mask): You envelop yourself in an illusion that makes you appear nonthreatening and harmless. Strikes gain an edge; +1 bonus distance to Disengage. Ends when Lew harms a creature. Spend 1 insight: appear as a specific creature within 10 squares with an edge on tests to pass as them.
  • Careful Observation (Maneuver, Ranged 20): Assess a target. As long as Lew maintains line of effect and doesn’t strike another creature first, his next strike gains an edge and 1 surge.
Heroic Abilities
  • Clever Trick (1 Insight, Triggered, Magic): Trigger: An enemy targets Lew with a strike. Choose an enemy within the strike’s distance (including the attacker). The strike targets that enemy instead.
  • So Gullible (5 Insight, Free Triggered, Magic): Trigger: A creature targets Lew with a strike. Use Clever Trick for free, teleport 3 squares, make a free strike, and spend a Recovery.
  • You Talk Too Much (9 Insight, Melee 1 or Ranged 5, Agility): 10/15/21 + Agility damage; target is dazed (save ends, Presence potency) and can’t communicate with anyone until end of encounter.

Negotiation: Interest 6 · Patience 5
Motivations: Profit, Curiosity, Self-preservation
Pitfalls: Threats, Moralizing, Demanding charity

Director Notes: Lew’s Negotiation stats matter more than his combat stats—the PCs need to bargain with him, not fight him. If cornered, Lew uses Smoke Bomb to vanish, Crafty to move without provoking, and Clever Trick / So Gullible to punish anyone foolish enough to swing at him. He does not stand and fight. Use Keep It Down for covert tavern briefings and I’m No Threat for infiltration scenes.

Key Trait: Lew always names his price. And he always collects.

Mechanics to Use (GM)

Heat, Weight, and Word (Campaign Currencies)

Svellheim’s economy runs on three currencies:

  • Heat: Fuel, food, shelter, warmth. Measured in “ration-days” or “lantern-hours.”
  • Weight: Tools, weapons, repairs, transportation. Measured in iron-tokens, cart-days, or smith-hours.
  • Word: Reputation, contracts, oaths, legal standing. Measured in testimonies, warrants, or Thing-votes.

Use these as costs for Montage Tests, Negotiation leverage, and faction interactions.

Montage Tests: Travel as Consequences (Draw Steel, p. 261)

Every travel leg between major locations is a Montage Test. The heroes take turns making tests as they tackle the journey together across rounds. Each hero acts once per round (roll a test, assist another hero’s test, or use an ability/item). A hero cannot use the same skill twice across the montage. Two montage test rounds, then the test ends.

Montage Test Difficulty (base: 5 heroes)
DifficultySuccess LimitFailure Limit
Easy55
Moderate64
Hard73

Adjustments: For 4 or fewer heroes, decrease both limits by 1 per hero fewer than 5 (minimum 2). For 6+, increase both by 1 per extra hero.

Outcomes

  • Total Success: Hit the success limit before the failure limit. Goal achieved without complication. Earn 1 Victory (easy/moderate) or 2 Victories (hard).
  • Partial Success: Hit the failure limit or run out of rounds, but have at least 2 more successes than failures. Succeed with a complication or cost. Earn 1 Victory (moderate/hard).
  • Total Failure: Hit the failure limit or run out of rounds without the 2-success buffer. The goal is not achieved.
Individual Test Outcomes (p. 250)
RollEasyMediumHard
≤ 11Success w/ consequenceFailureFailure w/ consequence
12–16SuccessSuccess w/ consequenceFailure
17+Success w/ rewardSuccessSuccess
Nat 19–20Success w/ rewardSuccess w/ rewardSuccess w/ reward

Skills grant +2 to the test (not an edge). Edges and banes apply as normal (+2/−2; double edge/bane shifts tier by 1).

Rewards and Consequences: Handle individually per test. Default rewards = hero tokens. Default consequences = the Director gains additional Malice in the next combat encounter.

Negotiation Encounters

Use Draw Steel’s Negotiation structure for:

  • Recruiting Kaelen (Phase 2) — Interest 5, Patience 4. Motivations: redemption, duty, protecting common folk. Pitfalls: glory, orders from authority, minimizing her guilt.
  • Bargaining with Lew (Phase 1) — Interest 6, Patience 5. Motivations: profit, curiosity, self-preservation. Pitfalls: threats, moralizing, demanding charity.
  • Gaining access to the Steam-Oasis (Phase 4)
  • Faction politics at the Thing (Phase 3)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

After each travel montage or major faction scene, make a secret d6 check. This represents Grafvitnir’s network acting on intelligence relayed by Dreyfus from the king’s side:

  • 1–3: No network activity this phase. Intelligence is delayed or agents are out of position.
  • 4–5: Grafvitnir’s agents execute a complication: a social obstacle (warrant, testimony, bribe from a corrupted official), a sabotage of Heat (spoiled stores, stolen lantern oil by Pale Maw operatives), or an ambush on bad footing (agents acting on Dreyfus’s intelligence).
  • 6: A Pale Maw agent or Wyrd-touched creature intercepts directly (ambush, forced confrontation, or dramatic reveal).

Adjust the die range as the campaign progresses. By Phase 4, Grafvitnir’s network should be almost certain to act.

Level: 1
Duration: ½ session
Locations: The PCs' home towns
Type: Roleplay introduction, table-round character moments
Tone: Grim duty, communal desperation, old law invoked in new crisis

Overview

Before the campaign’s main machinery begins, the ancient call goes out under High King Harald’s Danelaw: a Konungs-ferð—a king’s-journey duty, invoked in desperation. The last time this law was used was three generations ago, during the Second Barrow-War. Its invocation now tells every jarl in Svellheim that the king is out of options.

The call reaches multiple towns (the PCs' homes) through riders, skalds, stamped notices, or lantern signals. Each community chooses (or pressures) a local hero to answer. The chosen are told to make for Hrafnborg—because contracts, rations, and authority still exist there.

This beat establishes:

  • Svellheim is freezing. The world is ending slowly.

  • The PCs are not chosen for glory—they are chosen because someone had to go.

  • The law still has weight, even when everything else is failing.

The Call

Run this as a quick table-round introduction. Each player describes:

  • How the Konungs-ferð reached their character’s town (rider, skald, lantern signal, sealed letter with the raven-seal).

  • How their community "volunteered" them (Thing vote, hearth-oath called in, a debt owed, a Quiet Warden’s quiet suggestion, a parent’s sacrifice).

  • What they packed (one personal item that matters to them—this becomes a character hook later).

The call is formal, ancient, and heavy with law:

"By the Danelaw of High King Harald, bearer of the Cold Iron Crown, a Konungs-ferð is proclaimed. Let each settlement of loyal standing send forth one champion to Hrafnborg, there to serve the realm’s need in this time of shadow and frost."

The words are old. The desperation is new.

Running the Selection

Let the table roleplay why each hero was chosen. Give each player 2–3 minutes to set their scene.

Prompts for the local jarl or hall-leader:

  • "You’re too good to waste here. Go make my name bigger."

  • "If you die in the fjords, I won’t have to feed you."

  • "You owe me for the winter I saved you from. This is the price."

  • "They asked for a warrior. I’m sending a problem."

  • "You’re the only one who can speak to strangers without starting a blood-feud."

  • "The Warden said your name. I don’t argue with the Wardens."

What the PCs Know

  • The Fimbulwinter is three years old and getting worse.

  • The dead are rising—not everywhere, but more often, and closer to towns.

  • Hrafnborg is the largest settlement still standing. The king is there. Rations are there. Law is there.

  • A Konungs-ferð is a serious thing. Refusing it stains a family’s name for a generation.

What the PCs Don’t Know

  • Why the Konungs-ferð was really called.

  • That the Ever-Embers are dying.

  • That the Ember-Keepers are dead.

  • That they are being chosen specifically because they are unknown—outsiders the king can trust because no one in his court has had time to compromise them.

GM Notes

📝 Pacing and Tone

This beat should feel like the beginning of a journey no one wants to make. The PCs are not heroes yet—they are burdens their communities can afford to lose, debts that needed paying, or good people in bad circumstances who couldn’t say no.

Do not rush this. The emotional foundation of the campaign is laid here. When the PCs look back on this moment from Beat 9, standing outside a besieged Hrafnborg, the contrast should hit like a hammer.

If a player struggles with the "why me?" prompt, offer one: "Your community’s healer says the dead whisper your bloodline’s name. Better you go than they come here."

📝 Connecting to Character Options

If your players chose Svellheim-specific ancestries, cultures, or careers during character creation, tie the Konungs-ferð to those choices:

  • Hearthborn: The local Ever-Ember flickered when the call arrived. The elders took it as a sign.

  • Frostblood: The community doesn’t trust what you are. Sending you away solves two problems.

  • Kin of the Deep: Something stirred in the old mine. The foreman says you know too much to stay.

  • Warden-touched: A Quiet Warden visited your door at dawn. "The dead are choosing sides. Time you did too."

Transition to Beat 2

Once each player has described their departure, compress time:

You leave behind the only warmth you’ve ever known. The roads are bad—worse than last year, worse than the year before. The mile-markers are buried. The lanterns are dark. But the direction is clear: south and west, toward the coast, toward Hrafnborg.

You are not the only one on the road.

The PCs begin to encounter each other on the Lantern Road. Proceed to Beat 2: The Road to Hrafnborg.

Level: 1
Duration: ½–1 session
Locations: The Lantern Road, a wayside inn (optional), a graveyard ruin outside Hrafnborg
Type: Travel montage (Easy), social introduction, combat encounter
Tone: Cold fellowship, growing dread, first blood

Overview

The PCs travel to Hrafnborg along the failing Lantern Road. On the way, they meet each other—strangers bound by the same grim duty. They share a road, a fire, and their suspicions.

On the last night, just outside Hrafnborg’s outer walls, the party passes an old church ruin with a frozen graveyard. The dead rise. This is the PCs' first combat encounter and their first proof that the crisis is real.

This beat establishes:

  • The PCs as a group (from suspicion to necessity).

  • Travel in Svellheim is dangerous and expensive.

  • The dead are rising, and they remember.

Part 1: The Road

Run the journey as a short Montage Test (Easy difficulty, 2–3 days of travel).

Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Easy (adjusted) 4 4

Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate the Lantern Road Reason Stay on failing roads, read buried markers, avoid dead-end detours.
Endure the cold Might Resist frostbite, carry gear through snowdrifts, ford frozen streams.
Find shelter at night Intuition Locate wind-breaks, abandoned crofts, or natural shelters.
Avoid trouble on the road Agility Slip past bandit camps, desperate refugees, or rival groups.

Montage Outcomes

  • Total Success: Arrive rested and supplied. No Heat loss. Earn 1 Victory.

  • Partial Success: Arrive tired. Lose 1 day’s rations (Heat) or one piece of minor equipment is damaged (Weight).

  • Total Failure: Arrive exhausted and rattled. One PC has a condition (frostbitten or exhausted). Lose 2 days' rations. Director gains 2 additional Malice for the graveyard encounter.

Part 2: Meeting on the Road

During the montage, the PCs encounter each other. Let them introduce themselves in character.

Start with suspicion, necessity, and shared hardship. Maybe they camp together. Maybe they share a fire at a roadside waystation.

Conversation starters:

  • "You answering the Konungs-ferð too? You poor bastard."

  • "I’ve got half a fire. You’ve got half a fire. Together that’s almost warm."

  • "Where are you from? …Never heard of it. Probably still standing, then."

Optional: The Burnt Peat (Tavern Introduction)

If you want a classic "you all meet in a tavern" opener, place a waystation inn halfway to Hrafnborg.

  • Name: The Burnt Peat

  • Warmth costs extra. (1 iron-token for a seat near the fire.)

  • The locals are wary of anyone answering the Konungs-ferð. ("You poor bastards. The king’s scraping the barrel now.")

  • The PCs can share a table, a fire, and their reasons for coming.

This gives you a social scene before the violence. It’s also a chance to introduce minor color NPCs (a Lantern Road courier, an Ashwright peat-seller, a Quiet Warden who says nothing but watches the PCs with unsettling calm).

Part 3: The Graveyard (Combat Encounter)

On the second or third night, just outside Hrafnborg’s outer walls, the party passes an old church ruin. The structure is half-collapsed. The graveyard is frozen, the headstones cracked and leaning.

The ruin looms against the grey sky—stone ribs of a long-dead faith. The graveyard sprawls around it: tilted stones, frozen earth, and something wrong in the air.

The cold here is different. It doesn’t just chill your skin. It pulls.

Then you hear it: the scrape of bone on frozen ground.

Encounter: Draugr Ambush

Difficulty: Moderate (Level 1 PCs)
Encounter Value: Tune to party size (3 heroes ≈ EV 12–16)

Enemies:

  • 3–4 Draugr Thralls (Level 1 Minions)

  • 1 Barrow-Warden (Level 2 Standard)

Terrain:

  • Frozen ground (difficult terrain in patches)

  • Cracked headstones (cover, 1 square high)

  • Ruined church walls (partial shelter, 2 squares wide, line-of-sight blocker)

Lighting: Dim (torches/lanterns required; attacks without a light source have a bane)

Special Rule: The Dead Remember the Law

When a draugr is reduced to 0 HP, it whispers a name or an accusation before crumbling. This is narrative color, but it foreshadows later revelations:

  • "You broke guest-right…"

  • "The Ember died because of you…"

  • "Kaelen’s name is ash…"

  • "The wolf waits beneath the root…"

Let the PCs react. Do not explain these whispers. They are seeds planted for Beats 7, 8, and 9.

Stat Blocks

Draugr Thrall (Level 1 Minion)

Undead, Medium

Stamina: 1 (drops at 1 damage) | Speed: 4

Attacks:

  • Frozen Grasp (Melee): +3 vs. Might; 3 damage and target is slowed until end of their next turn.

Special: Unfeeling—Immune to fear, charm, and pain-based effects.

Appearance: Frost-blue skin, tattered burial shrouds, hollow eyes that glow faint green.

Barrow-Warden (Level 2 Standard)

Undead, Medium

Stamina: 28 | Speed: 5

Attacks:

  • Rune-Blade (Melee): +4 vs. Might; 6 damage and target takes ongoing 2 cold damage (save ends).

  • Barrow-Call (Action, 3 Malice): Summon 2 Draugr Thralls in adjacent squares.

Special:

  • Law of the Grave—The Barrow-Warden can speak. It accuses the PCs of breaking oaths, desecrating graves, or "forgetting the old ways."

  • Unfeeling—Immune to fear, charm, and pain-based effects.

Appearance: Taller than the thralls, wearing rusted chain and a burial crown. Carries a @UUID[Item.SVH0FrostBlade01]{Frost-Etched Barrow-Blade}.

Aftermath

After the fight:

  • Let the PCs search the bodies. They find a @UUID[Item.SVH0BurialCoin01]{Ember-Keeper's Burial Coin} stamped with the ember-mark (a seven-pointed flame). If any PC recognizes it (medium Reason test), they know it’s an Ember-Keeper’s token—old, worn, and shouldn’t be in a common graveyard.

  • One PC might be touched by the Barrow-Warden’s accusation. ("You will fail them, too…") This is narrative, not mechanical—but let it land.

  • The PCs can see Hrafnborg’s walls in the distance. Lights. Smoke. Bells.

⚔️ Loot

  • @UUID[Item.SVH0BurialCoin01]{Ember-Keeper's Burial Coin} — Found on the Barrow-Warden’s body. A medium Reason test identifies it as an Ember-Keeper’s token.
  • @UUID[Item.SVH0FrostBlade01]{Frost-Etched Barrow-Blade} — Taken from the Barrow-Warden. The runes are Old Norrœnt and reference “the law of the grave.”

The last draugr crumbles to frost and ash. For a moment, the only sound is your breathing and the wind through the ruin’s ribs.

Then, from the south, you hear bells. Hrafnborg.

You’ve arrived.

Transition to Beat 3

The PCs approach Hrafnborg’s outer gates. They are processed by Chainwarden clerks—names taken, debts recorded, Konungs-ferð papers stamped. They are directed to modest quarters in a fortified hall. Fed. Told to wait.

The waiting feels wrong. Proceed to Beat 3: The Quiet Summons.

Level: 1
Duration: 1 session
Location: Hrafnborg — the King’s quarters
Key NPCs: High King Harald, Gragnir (Ash-Walker), Dreyfus (the hound)
Type: Roleplay, lore delivery, mission briefing
Tone: Paranoia, secrecy, the weight of impossible choices

Overview

The PCs have arrived in Hrafnborg after the graveyard fight. They expect processing, maybe assignments, perhaps even rest. Instead, they are kept in polite custody all day. Late in the evening, High King Harald arrives personally—no heralds, no guards—and leads them to his private sitting room.

Inside, with only a grey hound as witness, Harald reveals the truth: the Ever-Embers are dying, the Keepers are murdered, and Svellheim’s warmth is failing.

Gragnir appears from the shadows with the first step: find Lew Tosferd in Stairwatch, who can find Kaelen. The session ends with the PCs accepting the mission and Dreyfus having heard every word.

Part 1: Polite Custody

The PCs are ushered into a modest but warm chamber in one of Hrafnborg’s fortified halls. Guards bring food—salt pork, hard bread, watered ale—and stoke the brazier. They are told to rest. To wait. That the King will "receive them soon."

Hours pass. Morning becomes afternoon. Afternoon becomes dusk.

📝 GM Notes: Building Tension

This waiting period is meant to feel wrong. Let the players describe what their characters do. Some may try to leave—politely discourage them with guards who are unfailingly courteous but immovable.

"The King’s orders, friend. For your safety."

Let investigation checks (easy Reason or Intuition test) or passive observations reveal:

  • The guards change every two hours. They’re professional, wary, and won’t answer questions.

  • No one else is being treated this way. Other Konungs-ferð arrivals are processed through the Chainwardens' ledgers and released into the city.

  • The PCs can hear distant alarm bells twice during the day—brief, then silenced. (These are wall attacks the King will mention later.)

Pacing tip: If the players get restless, compress time. "Another hour passes. Then another. The light outside turns grey, then dark. And then—the door opens."

Part 2: The King Comes

The door opens quietly. No fanfare. No herald.

A man steps inside—broad-shouldered, greying at the temples, wearing a simple wool tunic under a fur-lined cloak. His eyes are sunken, and his hands bear fresh ink stains. Behind him, the hallway is empty.

"Come," he says, voice low. "And speak softly."

He doesn’t wait for questions. He turns and walks.

This is High King Harald. PCs who grew up in Svellheim’s towns will recognize him from coins, carvings, or stories. He looks older than his reputation. Tired.

Let the PCs follow or hesitate. If they ask questions aloud, Harald puts a finger to his lips and gestures: follow.

The Walk

Harald leads them through cold stone corridors. PCs with sharp eyes (easy Reason or Intuition test) notice:

  • No other guards in this section.

  • The torches are spaced farther apart than usual.

  • The route avoids the main halls.

When they reach the King’s private door, there are no guards. This is unusual. In Hrafnborg, even minor nobles post at least one huscarl.

Harald unlocks the door himself, ushers the PCs inside, and locks it behind them.

Part 3: The Sitting Room

The room is warm—perhaps the warmest you’ve felt since the graveyard. A low fire burns in a stone hearth. Furs cover the floor. A table holds a map weighted with cold daggers. Shelves line the walls: scrolls, wax tablets, a child’s wooden sword.

The room is empty. No advisors. No guards. No servants.

On the floor near the fire, a large grey hound raises its head. Its eyes are pale, almost silver. It watches you without blinking.

Harald closes the door and leans against it. For a moment, he just breathes.

The hound is Dreyfus. Let the PCs react. If they try to speak to the dog, it does not respond—but it watches. If anyone uses magic, makes an oath aloud, or speaks a true name, Dreyfus’s ears perk.

⚠ GM Secret: Dreyfus the Listener

Dreyfus is a shape-walker—a fragment of Grafvitnir’s will given form. He is Wyrd-bound by guest-right and cannot harm anyone in this room, but he can hear, remember, and report.

For now, let him be an unnerving presence. Do not reveal anything yet. The PCs should feel that the dog is strange, but attribute it to an old animal’s quirks.

Tells (for observant players, hard Intuition test):

  • Dreyfus never blinks.

  • He watches mouths more than hands.

  • He does not eat from the bowl by the hearth (it’s been full for days).

  • When Harald speaks the word "Kaelen," the hound’s ears flatten—not in fear, but in attention.

Part 4: Harald’s Confession

Once the PCs are settled (or at least silent), Harald speaks. Use this as a framework, not a script—let the players interrupt, question, or react.

"The Ever-Ember spoke," Harald begins, his voice flat. "Three weeks ago. It spoke to the Keepers and said the Ever-Embers across Svellheim are dying. That it is tired. That it can no longer hold the boundaries between the living and the dead."

He moves to the table, touches the map. "It promised answers. Promised to explain, after it rested, how we might rekindle the flames. I brought the council together. We prepared. We waited."

He closes his eyes. "The night before the meeting, the Keepers were slain. All seven. In their beds. No signs of struggle. No witnesses."

His jaw tightens. "And the next morning, the dead came for our walls."

Let the PCs process. Then Harald continues:

  • The attacks have escalated: skeletons, ghosts, barrow-wights, all striking the walls at different points.

  • Every defensive plan the council makes is anticipated. The attacks avoid the traps, circumvent the patrols.

  • Harald suspects a traitor—or a leak—within his own command structure.

  • He cannot trust his own court. He cannot trust his own shadows.

Lore: The Ever-Embers and Their Keepers

The Ever-Embers are sanctioned hearth-flames that burn in major settlements across Svellheim. They are not merely fires—they are anchors, points where Yggdrasil’s warmth-flow stabilizes. In folklore, they "keep the dead in their graves" and "hold back the long dark."

The Ember-Keepers (civic office) are chosen every ten years in a sacred rite:

  1. Children of age (13) are paraded past the Ever-Ember during the winter solstice.

  2. The flame itself chooses seven by spitting a coal-ember onto their foreheads, leaving a permanent mark (a scar that glows faintly in darkness).

  3. The chosen seven serve for ten years, then are replaced.

Duties:

  • Maintain the flame’s ring enclosure

  • Manage fuel and ward-lines

  • Raise wind-breaks to protect the flame from storms

  • Speak to the flame on the city’s behalf (a rite performed in silence, with offerings of ash and memory)

The murders are not just political—they are existential. Without Keepers, the Ever-Ember cannot be safely questioned, fed, or calmed. And if the Ever-Ember dies, the dead will flood the city.

Part 5: The Secret Mission

After the PCs absorb the crisis, Harald delivers the mission.

"I called the Konungs-ferð for a reason," Harald says, looking each of you in the eye. "Not for warriors. Not for a war band. I needed people I could trust who no one would suspect. People who owe loyalty to their homes, not to my council."

He straightens. "I need you to find Kaelen. She was one of the seven Keepers. In her second year, she left—abandoned her vows, her mark, everything. No one knows why. No one knows where she went."

"Find her. Bring her back. If anyone can speak to the Ever-Ember now, it’s her."

Let the PCs ask questions. Harald answers honestly (remember: Harald does not lie):

  • Why Kaelen? She was the youngest Keeper ever chosen (age 13, the minimum). She served faithfully for a year, then vanished. Harald believes guilt or fear drove her away—but also believes she still carries the ember-mark and the knowledge required to speak to the flame.

  • What happened to her? No one knows. She left no message. Some think she fled to the Interior Belt. Some think she’s dead.

  • Why us? "Because no one is watching you. Because you’re not on any ledger. Because I can’t trust my own shadows anymore."

  • What about the attacks? "I have soldiers. I have walls. I can hold—for a while. What I don’t have is a Keeper."

  • What if she won’t come? Harald’s jaw tightens. "Convince her. Svellheim’s warmth depends on it."

Part 6: The Interruption — Gragnir

Just as the PCs agree (or object), the shadows move.

"He tells you some of the truth."

The voice comes from the corner, low and rough as ash scraped from stone. A figure steps into the firelight—tall, lean, wrapped in smoke-grey furs. His fingers are black with soot. The smell of burning peat follows him.

Harald’s hand goes to his hip (no sword), then drops. "Gragnir."

The man—Gragnir—nods to the King, then looks at you. "The King wants Kaelen. Good. You’ll need her. But the King doesn’t know where she is. I do. Or rather—I know who does."

Gragnir explains:

  • There is a man named Llewyellen Tosferd—"everyone calls him Lew."

  • Lew has a gift: he can find things that are lost. Through old contracts, favors, and a network of informants that spans the Interior Belt—and through other means he won’t discuss.

  • Lew is currently in Stairwatch, a waystation town just beyond Midgard’s Shield, near the Titan’s Stair pass.

  • It’s a hard trek—ten days through cold, bandit country, and failing routes (~200 miles across the Coastal Belt and Midgard’s Shield)—and the party must travel in secret.

Harald looks grim but doesn’t contradict Gragnir. Instead, he asks: "What’s the price?"

Gragnir smiles, ash crinkling at the corners of his mouth. "For you? Nothing, Highness. For them?" He looks at the PCs. "Lew will name his price when you find him. He always does."

📝 GM Notes: Gragnir’s Manner

Gragnir is not hostile, but he is not comforting either. He speaks like someone who has seen this crisis coming for years and is tired of being ignored.

If the PCs press him:

  • "How did you get in here?" — "The same way the cold gets in. Through the cracks."

  • "Why don’t YOU find Kaelen?" — "Because she’ll run from me. She won’t run from strangers. Not at first."

  • "What aren’t you telling us?" — "Everything I can’t prove yet. When I can, you’ll wish I hadn’t."

  • "Who are you?" — "A man who talks to trees. The trees are dying. That should concern you."

Part 7: The Kicker

As the meeting concludes, Gragnir fades back into the shadows (literally or metaphorically—your choice). Harald gives the PCs what aid he can:

  • Travel papers (forged as "merchants' retinue" to avoid Chainwarden scrutiny)

  • Supplies: 3 days' cold-weather rations, 2 lantern-oil flasks, 1 @UUID[Item.SVH0CipherPack01]{King’s Cipher Packet} (to be used only in dire need)

  • A warning: "Trust no one in my name. If someone quotes my orders, assume they’re lying or compromised."

As you turn to leave, the grey hound—Dreyfus—rises and pads silently to the door. He sits. He watches. And for just a moment, you’d swear the hound smiles.

⚠ GM Secret: The Leak Is Set

Dreyfus has heard everything. The mission. The names. The destination. He will remain at Harald’s side—listening to every war council, every messenger, every whispered order—and relay intelligence to Grafvitnir’s network through Wyrd-channels: root-whispers carried along Yggdrasil’s dying root-lines.

This is the engine that drives the Grafvitnir Pressure Check for the rest of Act 1. The PCs don’t know they’ve been compromised—but you do.

Narrative consequences for later beats:

  • Grafvitnir’s agents—Pale Maw cultists, corrupted officials, Wyrd-touched creatures—know the PCs' mission and destination.

  • Dreyfus staying with Harald means the spy has ongoing access: every update Harald receives about the PCs' progress flows straight to the enemy.

  • The PCs will not learn the source until Beat 8, when Gragnir reveals the truth.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Understood the existential crisis: Ever-Embers dying, Keepers dead, dead rising.

  • Accepted the mission: find Kaelen and bring her back.

  • Met Harald (desperate, honest, out of options) and Gragnir (cryptic, blunt, urgent).

  • Learned the next step: find Lew Tosferd in Stairwatch.

  • Felt the weight of secrecy and paranoia.

  • Been unknowingly compromised—Dreyfus heard every word and remains at Harald’s side, feeding intelligence to Grafvitnir’s network.

Transition to Beat 4

The PCs must leave Hrafnborg before dawn, using the forged papers to pass the Chainwardens' outer checkpoints. Harald cannot help them leave—his involvement must remain invisible.

Proceed to Beat 4: The Road to Stairwatch.

Level: 1
Duration: 1–2 sessions
Location: Hrafnborg (departure) → Lantern Road → Frostfen Marshes → Midgard’s Shield → Titan’s Stair → Stairwatch
Key NPCs: Warden-Sergeant Eirik Greyhand (Greypass), Bryn Ketilsdottir (Hollow Waystation)
Type: Two Hard Montage Tests, combat encounter, negotiation encounter, narrative interludes
Tone: Isolation, hardship, dwindling warmth, the road is the enemy

Overview

The PCs slip out of Hrafnborg before dawn with forged papers and the King’s supplies. Ahead: two hundred miles of failing roads, frozen marshland, Chainwarden checkpoints, mountain passes, and Fimbulwinter storms—all while maintaining the secrecy of their mission.

This is a long, dangerous journey. Travel rates vary with terrain: 24 miles/day on maintained roads, 18 miles/day through hindered ground (marshes, foothills), and 12 miles/day in severe mountain passes—averaging ~20 miles/day across the full route. The trek takes 8–10 days. The beat is divided into two montage tests separated by encounters, building a rhythm of endurance, danger, and discovery. By the time the PCs reach Stairwatch, they should feel every mile.

Part 1: The Departure

Sólmánuður 6th. Dawn.

You leave Hrafnborg before dawn, slipping through the Chainwardens' outer checkpoints with forged merchants' papers and the taste of salt pork still on your tongues. The Lantern Road stretches north into a world of grey ice and stunted pines.

The King’s instructions are clear: reach Stairwatch. Find Lew Tosferd. Trust no one.

The cold doesn’t care about instructions.

As the PCs pass through the outer checkpoint:

  • The Chainwarden on duty is half-asleep and waves them through with barely a glance. (This changes if the party dawdled or made noise in the King’s halls.)

  • The road beyond the walls is immediately different: the stones are cracked, weeds push through ice, and the mile-markers are buried or missing.

  • The sound of the city fades within minutes. The only sounds are wind, their own breathing, and the crunch of frost under boots.

📝 GM Notes: Leaving Hrafnborg

As the PCs pass through the outer checkpoint, note that Dreyfus is not here—he is back in Harald’s chambers. But every detail of the PCs' mission, destination, and route was spoken aloud in Harald’s sitting room while the hound listened.

Within hours of the PCs' departure, that intelligence begins flowing through Grafvitnir’s Wyrd-channels to agents in the field. The PCs don’t know this yet.

Part 2: Journey Context

Before beginning the first montage test, set the scene with a full description of the route:

Journey: Hrafnborg → Lantern Road north → Frostfen Marshes → Midgard’s Shield foothills → Chainwarden Toll at Greypass → Mountain Road → Titan’s Stair → Stairwatch descent
Distance: ~200 miles
Duration: ~8–10 days through Fimbulwinter terrain
Calendar: Departure Sólmánuður 6th; estimated arrival Sólmánuður 15th–16th
Terrain: Coastal Belt (frozen farmland) → Marshlands → Interior Belt boundary (Midgard’s Shield range)

The Route

  • Days 1–2 — The Lantern Road: Once a well-maintained highway connecting Hrafnborg to the interior towns. Now it’s a ruin: mile-markers buried, road-signs stolen for firewood, sections impassable with snow or debris. Chainwarden patrols tax travellers in Heat (fuel rations) or Word (travel permits). The land is flat, frozen farmland—open sky, cutting wind, and the Ever-Ember’s warmth fading with every mile.

  • Day 3 — The Frostfen Marshes: Where the Lantern Road crosses the old fenlands, the terrain turns treacherous. The marshes haven’t frozen solid—the ice is thin in patches, the reeds hide pockets of black water, and the footcauseway is half-collapsed. The road narrows to a single crumbling path through frozen bog. Something about the Frostfen feels wrong—the cold here is different, wetter, heavier, as if the land itself is exhaling.

  • Days 4–5 — The Refugee Road: Beyond the Frostfen, the Lantern Road meets the Herdweg—the old drovers' highway running east from the Interior Belt. The intersection is choked with refugees fleeing southwest toward Hrafnborg. Families, merchants, whole villages on the move. The PCs are heading northeast when everyone else is heading southwest. Questions will be asked.

  • Days 5–6 — The Chainwarden Toll at Greypass: Where the road enters the foothills of Midgard’s Shield, a fortified checkpoint controls passage. The Toll was built to manage trade; now it manages panic. Chainwardens in cold-bitten armour inspect papers, confiscate contraband, and demand tariffs that grow steeper every month. This is a negotiation encounter.

  • Days 6–8 — Midgard’s Shield Foothills: Rocky, wind-scoured ground. The treeline thins. Abandoned crofts and shepherds' shelters dot the landscape—some still have walls, none have roofs. The weather turns from "miserable" to "lethal." The road climbs steadily. The air thins. Storm-fronts roll in without warning.

  • Days 8–9 — The Mountain Road: The ancient road that climbs into the Titan’s Stair proper. Built by giants, if the stories are true—the flagstones are enormous, cracked but enduring. Avalanche debris, rockfalls, and ice-slick switchbacks make the ascent exhausting. A partially collapsed stone bridge spans a gorge halfway up.

  • Day 10 — Titan’s Stair & Descent: The final push: a narrow ice-choked switchback pass through the highest point of Midgard’s Shield. The wind is a physical force. The descent on the far side is steep but windless—a blessed relief—and Stairwatch’s lights are visible from the top.

What the PCs Notice

As they travel, seed these observations:

  • The Ever-Ember’s warmth faded fast once they left Hrafnborg’s radius. The cold deepens with every mile.

  • Roadside shrines to Yggdrasil appear every few miles—small cairns with hollow centres where flame-offerings should sit. Every single one is extinguished. Some have been for weeks.

  • Wildlife is wrong: no birds, no hares, no foxes. The only tracks are old. Something has driven everything south or underground.

  • The people they pass on the road don’t make eye contact. They walk fast, heads down, clutching whatever they can carry. No one stops to talk.

  • The sky doesn’t change colour. Dawn and dusk are the same shade of iron-grey. Only the light’s intensity tells you what time it is.

  • At one point, they pass a farmstead. No smoke from the chimney. The door is open. Snow has drifted inside. (If they investigate: the family left in haste. Food is still on the table—frozen solid.)

Part 3: Montage Test 1 — The Coastal Road

Covers Days 1–4: Hrafnborg → Lantern Road → Frostfen Marshes → Refugee Road junction.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds (each hero acts once per round; 8 total individual tests).

Rules Reminder:

  • Each hero uses a different skill for each test (no repeating skills across the montage).

  • Skills grant +2 to the test (not an edge). Edges and banes apply as normal.

  • If a hero has a clever non-test action (using an item, ability, or resource), it can earn automatic successes at your discretion.

  • A hero can assist another hero’s test instead of making their own. The assisting hero must use a different skill than the lead. Roll a power roll: tier 1 = bane on lead’s test; tier 2 = edge; tier 3 = double edge.

Round 1 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description

Read weather signs to avoid a squall Intuition A Fimbulwinter squall can drop visibility to arm’s length in minutes. Reading the cloud-patterns and wind-shift gives the party time to shelter before it hits.

Round 1 Interlude: The Extinguished Shrine

By the roadside, a cairn of stacked stones stands waist-high. Its centre is hollow—a bowl-shaped depression where a flame should burn. The ash is cold. Dead wildflowers, frost-brittle, lie scattered around the base. Someone left offerings. No one came to tend the fire.

The cairn’s keystone is carved with a single runic word. It reads: Remember.

A moment for the PCs to reflect on what the Ever-Embers mean. If anyone makes an offering (even just a breath warmed into their hands and placed in the basin), the cairn’s keystone warms—just for a moment—then goes cold again. Yggdrasil is listening, but too weak to answer.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Find shelter in the Frostfen margins Reason The flat marshland offers no natural shelter. Locate an old peat-cutter’s hut, an overturned barge, or a reed-bank thick enough to break the wind.
Cross the Herdweg junction without attention Presence / Agility The junction is crowded with refugee columns. The PCs need to cross through the flow heading northeast without drawing questions. Why are young, healthy travellers heading toward the danger?
Resist marsh-sickness Might The Frostfen’s air carries something foul—not disease, exactly, but a heaviness in the lungs. A character who fails wakes the next morning with a rattling cough and bloodshot eyes. (−1 to Might tests until a rest.)
Avoid refugee columns Presence / Agility Families fleeing southwest toward Hrafnborg clog the road. The PCs need to avoid drawing attention. Questions about their direction—heading northeast when everyone else flees southwest—will raise suspicion.
Calm a panicked pack-mule Presence The mule balks at a frozen corpse on the road and threatens to bolt with half the supplies.
Keep morale up at camp Presence The landscape is crushing. Four days of grey-white emptiness and freezing wind. The PCs need to keep each other from despair. Stories, songs, small comforts.

Round 2 Interlude: The Drowned Village

The Lantern Road crosses what was once a shallow lake—you can tell by the reed-stumps and the change in ground texture. At the lake’s centre, barely visible through frost-haze, rooftops jut from the ice. A village, flooded and frozen.

The ice is clear enough to see through in places. Below: a market square, complete with stalls and carts. A temple spire, its bell still visible. Streets and doorways and—

Movement. Something shifts below the ice. Not a fish. Something with hands.

The PCs should not investigate. If they do: the ice creaks ominously, and whatever is below tracks their movement across the surface. A easy Reason test identifies this as a draugr nest—the village died and the residents didn’t leave. Breaking the ice would release dozens of them.

If the PCs are wise enough to move on, they gain useful information: the dead are everywhere, not just in obvious battlefields and barrows. The Frostfen is a cemetery that doesn’t know it’s dead.

Montage 1 Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes before failure limit)

  • The party crosses the Frostfen and reaches the Herdweg junction intact.

  • Earn 2 Victories.

  • Supplies are holding. Morale is manageable.

  • Dreyfus’s intelligence from Hrafnborg hasn’t reached field agents yet—delay the first Grafvitnir Pressure Check by one scene.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • Arrive at the Herdweg junction, but at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Lost a day navigating the Frostfen. One day’s rations consumed for no progress. (−1 day’s supplies.)

    • Health cost: Marsh-sickness. One PC has a persistent cough (−1 to Might tests until a respite in a warm building).

    • Remedy available: PCs who forage successfully in the marshes (medium Intuition test) discover marsh-root — the raw ingredient for @UUID[Item.SVH0MarshPoult01]{Frostfen Marsh-Root Poultice}. An herbalist PC can prepare one during a rest.

    • Word cost: A refugee recognised the King’s seal on the forged papers and spread the word. The PCs' cover is thinner. (+1 to future Grafvitnir Pressure Checks.)

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party still reaches the junction, but arrives compromised:

    • Lose 2 days' rations and one PC has marsh-sickness.

    • Grafvitnir’s agents know exactly where the party is. The next Pressure Check triggers automatically at 5+.

    • No Victories earned. Director gains 3 Malice for the next encounter.

Montage 1 Failure Encounter: Chainwarden Patrol (Optional)

This encounter triggers on a Total Failure or at the Director's discretion after a Partial Success. A Chainwarden patrol has been tipped off by Ashwright informants or Pale Maw agents. They are waiting at a narrow bottleneck on the Herdweg approach—a stone bridge over a frozen ravine.

The road narrows ahead. A stone bridge spans a frozen creek bed—old stone, Jötunfolk work, still solid after centuries. On the far side, five figures in grey cloaks wait in a loose line. Chain links glint on their tabards. The lead figure raises a gauntleted hand.

“Halt. Toll authority, road inspection. Papers.”

The tone is wrong. This isn't bored routine. They were waiting for you.

📝 GM Notes

What's really happening: Warden-Captain Halvard received a raven from Ashwright's network with a description of the party. He's been told to detain them—alive, if possible, but he has latitude. Halvard is a pragmatist who has been bribed, not a zealot. If the fight goes badly, he'll try to retreat.

Negotiation possible: If the PCs try to talk their way out (medium Presence test), they can avoid combat—but only if they haven't already antagonised the Chainwardens at the toll. If they dealt with Eirik's toll peacefully, they get an edge on this test.

Part 4: Point of Interest — The Hollow Waystation

Between the first montage and the Chainwarden Toll, the party encounters this location. It can serve as a rest stop or a trap.

The road widens at a stone-built waystation—one of the old Embertender rest-houses that once dotted the major routes. The roof is intact. The walls are solid. Smoke rises from the chimney.

Someone is inside.

Inside: Bryn Ketilsdottir, a former Embertender who abandoned her post when her settlement’s Ever-Ember went out. She’s been living in the waystation for three weeks, burning furniture for warmth, rationing a dwindling supply of dried fish.

  • Bryn is not hostile, but she is deeply ashamed. She failed her oath. The Ember she tended flickered and died in the night—no attack, no catastrophe, it just…​ stopped. She couldn’t bear to face her community.

  • If the PCs offer compassion: Bryn shares what she knows. The Ever-Embers are going out without external cause. Three more have failed since hers—she heard the news from refugees on the road. "It’s not the cold killing them," she says. "It’s something underneath. The ground is sick."

  • If the PCs push her for information (easy Presence test): Bryn describes the night her Ember died. "I heard a sound. Not from outside—from below. Like something exhaling. The flame bent toward the floor and went out. The stone was warm for an hour afterward, as if the fire had sunk into the earth."

  • If the PCs share food: Bryn is overwhelmed. She insists on giving them her last useful possession—a Chainwarden toll-token (worth one free passage through a checkpoint, saving Heat or Word at the Greypass Toll).

  • If the PCs help Bryn secure the waystation (or if they show genuine kindness), she also offers a battered iron lantern from the waystation’s stores: the @UUID[Item.SVH00WayLantrn01]{Hollow Waystation Lantern}. “It was the Embertender’s before mine. The flame won’t die in the wind. Take it — I don’t deserve to carry their light.”

📝 GM Notes: Bryn

Bryn is not a quest-giver. She is a window into the world. Her story illustrates what Fimbulwinter does to ordinary people—it breaks them not through violence, but through grinding, inexorable failure. If the PCs are kind to her, they earn a toll-token and a contact who may reappear in Act 2 (she can reach Hrafnborg and serve the King). If they ignore her, she’s just another ghost on the road.

Part 5: Negotiation Encounter — The Chainwarden Toll at Greypass

Approximately Sólmánuður 10th. Day 5 of the journey.

Context

Where the Lantern Road enters the foothills of Midgard’s Shield, the Chainwardens maintain a fortified toll crossing called Greypass. It controls the only maintained road through the lower mountains. The toll was built to manage trade caravans; now it manages desperation.

The road narrows between two bluffs of dark stone. A wall of stacked timber and iron bars blocks the path—not a military fortification, but close enough. A gate. A guardhouse. A flag bearing the Chainwarden sigil: a broken chain, re-forged.

Two wardens stand at the gate, wrapped in furs over mail. One holds a ledger. The other holds a spear.

"Papers," says the one with the ledger. It is not a request.

The Encounter

This is a Moderate Negotiation with the toll commander, Warden-Sergeant Eirik Greyhand.

Interest Patience
5 4

Motivations:

  • Eirik wants order. He is not corrupt—he is exhausted. The toll is underfunded, understaffed, and overwhelmed. He wants travellers to follow procedure so he can maintain the fiction that the system works.

  • Eirik wants supplies. The toll garrison hasn’t received a resupply in weeks. Heat (fuel, rations) is more valuable to him than coin.

  • Eirik wants to go home. He has a family in Hrafnborg. He hasn’t heard from them in a month. Any news—even bad news—is worth something.

What the PCs Want:

  • Passage through the toll without their cover being blown.

  • Ideally, intelligence about the road ahead (mountain conditions, bandit activity, undead sightings).

  • If they have Bryn’s toll-token, they can present it for automatic free passage for one person. The rest must still negotiate.

Negotiation Beats

Argument Effect
Present the forged papers (Presence) Eirik inspects them. They’re good forgeries. Tier 2+: he’s satisfied. Tier 1: he notices the seal is slightly wrong—"This isn’t the standard merchant cipher. Where did you get these?" (+1 Interest, as he becomes suspicious but also curious.)
Offer rations or fuel (Heat currency) This speaks directly to Eirik’s need. Tier 2+: earns 2 Interest immediately. Eirik waves them through and offers road intelligence as thanks.
Share news of Hrafnborg (Presence) Tell Eirik the city still stands, the Ever-Ember still burns, and the King is active. Tier 2+: Eirik’s shoulders drop with relief. +1 Interest. He becomes notably friendlier.
Bluff a military connection (Presence) Claim to be on Chainwarden business. Tier 3 only: Eirik buys it and provides an escort for the next 5 miles. Tier 1–2: Eirik knows every Warden in the district. He doesn’t know you. −1 Patience.
Threaten or intimidate (Might) Catastrophic. Eirik has 8 wardens and a fortified position. −2 Patience. If Patience hits 0 through threats, the PCs are detained and must escape (adding a day to the journey and burning their cover).
Present Bryn’s toll-token Automatic: one party member passes free. Eirik recognises the token and asks after Bryn. If the PCs mention she’s alive and where she is, he is visibly relieved—"She was a good Tender. We all wondered."

Outcomes

Interest Achieved: The PCs pass freely. Eirik provides road intelligence:

"The mountain road is passable but ugly. Two rockfalls in the last week—you’ll need to detour around the second one, near Kross-stead. And watch the gorge bridge—the western footing has shifted. It’ll hold, probably, but don’t linger on it."

He pauses. "There’s also…​ something else. The patrols up in the high country have been finding tracks. Big ones. Not bear, not wolf. Something that walks on two legs but drags something behind it. We don’t go looking. I’d suggest you don’t either."

If the PCs impressed Eirik during the negotiation (Tier 2+ result), he also offers a confiscated weapon from his evidence locker: the @UUID[Item.SVH00ChainDagr01]{Chainwarden’s Confiscated Dagger}. “You’ll need this more than my paperwork does.”

Patience Exhausted: The PCs are treated with suspicion. Choose one: They’re forced to surrender 2 days' rations as "toll payment" (Heat cost). Their descriptions are logged in the ledger. Grafvitnir’s network may access this. (+1 to all future Grafvitnir Pressure Checks.) ** They’re delayed half a day while paperwork is "processed" (Time cost).

Part 6: Interlude — The Frozen Messenger

Place this scene on the mountain road between the toll and the second montage.

A figure sits against a roadside boulder, hunched in a cloak. Snow has built up against one shoulder like a small drift. Their skin is blue-grey, their eyes open and glazed with frost.

A leather satchel hangs from one frozen hand.

The body is a courier—a Chainwarden runner, by the badge. Dead perhaps four days. The satchel contains:

  • A wax-sealed letter addressed to "The Watch-Commander, Stairwatch" — the seal is the King’s cipher, but not Harald’s personal seal. Someone else in the court sent this. The letter warns Stairwatch to "detain any travellers matching [the PCs' descriptions] and hold them for questioning."

  • 2 days' rations (frozen solid, but edible if thawed).

  • A small Chainwarden map of the Titan’s Stair pass, annotated with hazard marks. (+1 edge on one navigation test in the next montage.)

This is evidence that the leak in Hrafnborg is real. Someone besides Harald knows the PCs left. The question: is this the work of a mortal traitor, or has something in Harald’s court been passing information to forces outside the walls?

📝 GM Notes

Leave this ambiguous. The letter was generated by Dreyfus’s influence—intelligence whispered through Wyrd-channels from the King’s chambers to a compromised court official—but the PCs cannot know that yet. It could equally be a well-meaning functionary acting on incomplete orders.

If the PCs take the letter, they can use it in Stairwatch—either to bluff their way past officials (claiming the courier was delayed and they are the escort) or to warn Lew that Hrafnborg’s security is compromised.

  • Darkness Beyond Firelight: Creatures outside the campfire’s light radius have concealment. The wisps exploit this—they hover at the edge of visibility.

Aftermath

The draugr fall—one collapsing like a puppet with cut strings, the other crumbling into hoarfrost that melts in the fire’s heat. The wisps scatter like sparks and blink out.

Silence. The fire crackles. The wind moans through the broken walls.

Nobody sleeps well after that.

If the PCs examine the draugr remains (easy Reason test): these aren’t recent dead. They’re old—centuries old, based on the corroded armour fragments and the archaic brooch-style. Whatever is raising the dead isn’t just waking recent corpses. It’s reaching deep.

Part 8: Point of Interest — Kross-stead

Place this on the mountain road, roughly Day 7–8.

The road passes through what was once a mining settlement—a dozen stone buildings clustered around a shaft-head, with a crude smelter and a Chainwarden outpost. The outpost flag still flies, but there’s no one here.

The shaft-head is open. Warm air rises from the opening.

Kross-stead was a silver mine. The miners evacuated three weeks ago when the temperature in the lower shafts began rising—slowly, inexplicably. The Chainwarden garrison followed.

  • If the PCs investigate the shaft (not recommended, but PCs are PCs): warm, sulphurous air. The walls of the shaft are veined with something that isn’t silver—a dark, root-like growth that pulses faintly with amber light. This is connected to Yggdrasil’s root system, though the PCs can’t know that yet. Anyone who touches the growth feels a vibration—like a heartbeat, very slow, very deep.

  • If the PCs search the buildings: 1 day’s rations, a miner’s pickaxe (decent weapon), and a Chainwarden logbook. The last entry reads: "The walls of the lower gallery are warm to the touch. Supervisor Alrek says it’s geothermal. Alrek is an idiot. Trees don’t grow underground."

  • If the PCs take rope, pitons, or climbing gear from the mining stores: +1 edge on one Agility test at Titan’s Stair.

Part 9: Montage Test 2 — Midgard’s Shield & Titan’s Stair

Covers Days 5–10: Chainwarden Toll → Mountain Road → Titan’s Stair → Descent to Stairwatch.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds.

Round 3 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate around the rockfalls Reason Eirik warned about two rockfalls. The first is minor (scramble over rubble). The second, near Kross-stead, requires a full detour through an unmarked goat-track.
