Round 3 Interlude: The Watched Feeling

As you crest a hill, a feeling settles over the group—a prickling at the back of the neck. Not a sound. Not a movement. Just the unmistakable sense that something out there knows where you are.

The ridge to the northwest is empty. The sky is grey. Nothing moves.

But the feeling doesn’t leave.

This is the first effect of Grafvitnir’s network. Intelligence from Hrafnborg has reached agents in the field, and the PCs are now being tracked—not by the hound, but by the Pale Maw’s informant chains: corrupted couriers, compromised Chainwarden posts, and Wyrd-touched carrion birds with silver eyes circling high above.

If anyone uses magic to scan: nothing registers. Grafvitnir’s agents are mortal. The Wyrd-channels that feed them are invisible to arcane detection.

Round 4 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Traverse the Titan’s Stair switchbacks Agility Ice-slick switchbacks carved into the mountain face. The path is narrow—two abreast at best—with a sheer drop on one side. One slip means a very long fall.
Keep the group moving through exhaustion Presence Day 9. Everyone is tired, cold, and running low on supplies. The Stair is the hardest section yet, and moral strength matters as much as physical.
Handle the ice-bridge at the summit Agility / Reason The final crossing before the descent: a natural ice-bridge formed over the top of the pass. It groans underfoot. Characters can test the ice (Reason) to find the strongest path or trust balance (Agility) and move fast.
Descend safely to the Interior Belt Agility The descent from Titan’s Stair is steep and loose—scree, ice-melt channels, and treacherous footing. A controlled descent requires skill; a rushed one invites broken ankles.
Scout for threats on the descent Intuition The far side of the pass opens into the Interior Belt. From the heights, the PCs can see Stairwatch below—and potentially spot anyone waiting for them.
Forage for emergency supplies Intuition Supplies running low? The high country offers slim pickings: mountain hare, snowberry bushes, frozen lichen. Enough to survive, not to thrive.

Round 4 Interlude: The Cairn Field

Near the summit of Titan’s Stair, the path widens into a flat plateau. Cairns stand here—dozens of them, rough-stacked stone pillars ranging from knee-height to taller than a man. Each one is marked with runes.

These are travel-cairns—monuments left by generations of travellers who survived the crossing. The tradition: add a stone to the pile, speak a name, and the crossing grants safe passage.

Some of the cairns are recent. One is barely a day old—a small stack with a child’s mitten tucked between the stones.

The newest cairn has no runes. Just a word scratched into the topstone with a knife:

HELP.

No mechanical effect, but this scene hammers home the stakes: this road is barely passable even for the desperate, and the PCs are walking it by choice. If any PC adds a stone to the cairn field, the wind drops for exactly one minute—long enough to feel the sun’s warmth through the clouds, faint but real.

Part 10: Montage 2 Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes before failure limit)

  • The party clears Titan’s Stair and descends to the Interior Belt intact.

  • Earn 2 Victories (cumulative with Montage 1; maximum 4 Victories from both).

  • Arrive in Stairwatch with supplies and energy.

  • The PCs have proven themselves on the road. They are harder now than when they left.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • Arrive in Stairwatch at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Lose 2 days' rations navigating around the rockfalls. Supplies are now critical.

    • Weight cost: One PC’s equipment is damaged crossing the Gorge Bridge or the ice-bridge (weapon, armour, or tool needs repair in Stairwatch—costs Weight).

    • Word cost: The PCs were spotted by someone on the descent—a distant figure who turned back toward the toll. Their passage is now on record. (+1 to future Grafvitnir Pressure Checks.)

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party still reaches Stairwatch, but arrives wrecked:

    • Lose remaining rations and one piece of equipment is broken.

    • Grafvitnir’s agents know exactly where the party is. The next Pressure Check triggers automatically at 5+.

    • One PC arrives with a condition (exhausted, frostbitten, or rattled—choose based on the worst failure).

    • No Victories earned. Director gains 3 Malice for the next combat encounter.

Montage 2 Failure Encounter: Goblin Ambush (Optional)

This encounter triggers on a Total Failure or at the Director's discretion after a Partial Success. Goblins—thought extinct in the Titanspine for generations—have emerged from deep mountain caves, driven forward by an unknown power. They are desperate, feral, and organised under a self-styled monarch.

The descent path switchbacks through a narrow defile—loose scree above, a sheer drop below. Ahead, the trail is blocked by a crude barricade of broken cart wheels and piled stones.

Then the chittering starts.

Small shapes scramble along the cliff walls above you. Green-grey skin, yellow eyes, crude weapons. One of them—slightly larger, wearing a battered crown made from bent nails and copper wire—stands on a boulder and shrieks something in a language you don't understand.

It points at you. The others charge.

Goblins. Here. That shouldn't be possible.

📝 GM Notes

What's really happening: These goblins have been driven from their deep caverns by something below—the same power that's raising the dead and pulling things southwest. They don't know what it is. They're as scared as they are dangerous. The Goblin Monarch is desperately trying to establish territory for its tribe before winter kills them all.

Negotiation difficult but possible: A PC who speaks Caelian or can communicate through gestures can attempt to parley (hard Presence test). Success means the goblins let them pass in exchange for food (half the party's remaining rations). The Monarch is pragmatic—it wants its people alive more than it wants a fight.

Part 11: Arrival at Stairwatch

Approximately Sólmánuður 15th–16th.

Regardless of montage outcomes, the PCs arrive at Stairwatch.

Stairwatch hunches at the foot of Titan’s Stair like a dog that’s been kicked too many times. It’s a waystation town—built for travellers who never stay long, and it shows. The buildings lean. The streets are mud frozen into ridges. Smoke rises from every chimney, but the fires burn low.

The inn-sign reads The Broken Stair. Below it, smaller: Rooms. Ale. No questions.

The town’s Ever-Ember burns in a stone ring at the central crossroads. It’s the smallest you’ve seen—barely a campfire. But it burns. It burns.

What the PCs Find in Stairwatch

  • Population: ~300 permanent residents, plus transient miners, traders, and refugees.

  • Atmosphere: Tense, suspicious, but functional. People here don’t starve—they trade in ore from the Titan’s Stair mines—but they don’t trust outsiders.

  • The Broken Stair Inn: Run by a Dwarf named Hild. She asks no questions, charges fair prices, and waters the ale exactly enough to be annoying.

  • The Chainwarden Post: A small stone building near the Ever-Ember. Two Wardens: one bored, one paranoid. They’ll want to see papers.

  • Lew Tosferd: Not immediately visible. He doesn’t advertise. He lives somewhere in the upper town. Finding him is Easy because he’s well-known around town—but convincing him to help is another matter. (See Beat 5.)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

Roll the Grafvitnir Pressure Check (d6) upon arrival in Stairwatch. This represents Grafvitnir’s network—not Dreyfus himself, who remains in Hrafnborg—acting on relayed intelligence.

Roll Effect
1-2 No network activity. Intelligence from Hrafnborg hasn’t reached local agents yet. The PCs have breathing room.
3-4 A minor complication: a stranger asks too many questions at the inn, or someone follows the PCs to their lodging—a local informant paid by the Pale Maw’s network.
5 A flock of carrion-birds with silver eyes circles the town as the PCs arrive. They circle twice, then fly southwest toward Hrafnborg. Someone is watching from above.
6 Direct interception: the Chainwarden post has received a raven from Hrafnborg with updated descriptions of the PCs. They are detained at the gate for questioning. (This is a roleplay encounter—the PCs can talk or bluff their way out.)

Transition to Beat 5

The PCs are in Stairwatch. Ten days of hard road behind them. Two hundred miles of cold, corpses, and crumbling infrastructure.

They need to find Lew Tosferd and convince him to help locate Kaelen.

Proceed to Beat 5: Finding Lew.

Level: 1 → 2 (level-up occurs at end of this beat)
Duration: 1–2 sessions
Location: Stairwatch
Key NPCs: Llewyellen "Lew" Tosferd, Hild (innkeeper), Solvi (Ember-Keeper)
Type: Investigation, Negotiation encounter, local tasks
Tone: Noir-tinged, transactional, a town held together by stubbornness

Overview

The PCs have arrived in Stairwatch. Their objective is simple: find Lew Tosferd and convince him to locate Kaelen. The problem: Lew doesn’t work for free, he doesn’t trust strangers, and he has his own troubles in Stairwatch that need solving before he’ll consider leaving.

This beat introduces the party’s second major NPC ally, reveals the backstory connection between Lew and Gragnir, and ends with Lew’s finding-gift pointing the way to the Ashen Woods.

Part 1: Stairwatch at a Glance

Stairwatch is not a city. It’s a waystation that forgot to die.

The Town

  • Population: ~300 permanent, plus transients.

  • Economy: Ore from the Titan’s Stair mines, re-sold to traders heading to Hrafnborg or the Interior Belt. Some trapping, some charcoal-burning. Everything else is imported and expensive.

  • Architecture: Stone foundations, timber upper floors, turf roofs. Most buildings lean. Some lean together, forming accidental archways over the narrow streets.

  • The Ever-Ember: Burns in a stone ring at the central crossroads. It’s small—barely a campfire—but it’s warm. Locals gather near it at dusk and dawn. The Keeper here is a frightened teenager named Solvi who was chosen only six months ago.

  • Mood: People here don’t starve, but they don’t sleep well either. The mine produces enough iron to trade, but the miners report strange sounds deep in the tunnels. Three families left last month. Two more are packing.

Key Locations

Location Description
The Broken Stair Inn The only inn. Run by Hild, a Dwarf who asks no questions, charges fair prices, and waters the ale just enough to be annoying. Three rooms upstairs, a common hall with a fire that’s always burning, and a kitchen that smells of smoked mutton.
The Chainwarden Post A small stone building near the Ever-Ember. Two Wardens: Sergeant Tormund (bored, competent, drinks) and Deputy Freja (paranoid, by-the-book, doesn’t drink). They want to see papers.
The Mine Head A gated tunnel entrance at the north end of town. Currently half-operational. Miners go in pairs and don’t work past noon. The deep shafts are sealed—"bad air," they say.
The Upper Town A cluster of older stone buildings on the hill above the crossroads. Better-built, quieter. Lew’s lodging is here—a rented room above a chandler’s shop.
The Bone Market Not bones—the name is old. An open-air market near the gate where trappers sell furs, miners sell raw ore, and everyone trades rumors. Meets every other day.

Part 2: Finding Lew

Lew is not hiding, exactly. He’s just not advertising.

How the PCs Can Find Him

  • Ask at the Broken Stair: Hild knows Lew. "The Finder? Upstairs room, when he bothers to sleep here. More likely at the chandler’s. He owes me three nights' lodging." She’ll point them to the Upper Town.

  • Ask at the Bone Market: Any trader knows Lew. "The one-eyed man? Aye, he found my missing mule last winter. Charged me more than the mule was worth. Try the chandler’s."

  • Ask at the Chainwarden Post: Tormund knows him. "Tosferd? He’s useful. Keeps to himself. Doesn’t cause trouble I have to write up." Freja is more suspicious: "He knows too many people. Nobody knows that many people honestly."

  • Just walk the Upper Town: A man sits on a bench outside the chandler’s shop, whittling a piece of bone. One eye—the left—is real, dark, sharp. The right eye catches the light wrong: it gleams faintly amber, like a coal seen through fog. He watches you approach with the calm patience of someone who’s been expecting you.

First Meeting

"Three strangers with road-dust on their boots and a look like someone’s given them a job they didn’t ask for." The man doesn’t look up from his whittling. The knife moves in small, precise strokes. "Let me guess—someone sent you. Someone important. And they told you I could find something."

He looks up. One eye is dark and human. The other glints amber in the grey light—not glass, not quite stone. Something else.

"Name’s Lew. Sit down. You’re making the chandler nervous."

Lew is friendly, casual, and already three steps ahead. He knows about the Konungs-ferð (common knowledge), suspects these aren’t ordinary travelers (uncommon knowledge), and will listen to their pitch before naming his price.

📝 GM Notes: Playing Lew

Lew is not an obstacle—he’s a transaction. He genuinely wants to help, in the way a merchant genuinely wants to sell you something. He’s warm, funny, and utterly without sentiment about money.

Key behaviors:

  • He asks questions constantly—about the PCs, their homes, their skills. This is both genuine curiosity and professional assessment.

  • He never lies, but he tells you exactly as much as he’s decided to tell you. No more.

  • He refers to his finding-gift obliquely: "I have a knack," "I know people," "things turn up." He does not explain the eye unless pressed far enough.

  • He is never rude. He is always expensive.

Part 3: The Negotiation

This is a Negotiation Encounter using Draw Steel’s negotiation rules.

Setup

Interest: 6 — Lew is interested in the job. He just needs proper compensation.
Patience: 5 — He’ll hear them out, but he’s not patient with moralizing or demands.

Motivations (arguments that work):

  • Profit: Offer concrete payment—Heat, Weight, Word, favors, access. "What’s it worth to you?" is music to his ears.

  • Curiosity: Appeal to the mystery. Kaelen is the Keeper who vanished—finding her would be the greatest "lost thing" Lew’s ever tracked. "No one knows where she went. No one." This intrigues him deeply.

  • Self-preservation: The Ever-Embers are dying. If they go out, Stairwatch dies. Lew lives in Stairwatch. This is a hard argument for him to dismiss.

Pitfalls (arguments that backfire):

  • Threats: Lew does not respond to intimidation. He responds by becoming very helpful to your enemies.

  • Moralizing: "You have a duty" or "Think of the people" without concrete context makes his patience drop. He’s heard it all.

  • Demanding charity: "Do this for free because it’s important" is the fastest way to end the conversation. Lew always names his price.

Negotiation Flow

The PCs make arguments. Each argument is a power roll using an appropriate characteristic:

  • Presence for persuasion, flattery, or social pressure

  • Reason for logical arguments, evidence, or strategic framing

  • Intuition for reading Lew and tailoring the pitch to what he actually wants

Refer to Draw Steel’s Negotiation rules for test resolution.

What Lew Wants

If the PCs do well, Lew names his price. He wants two things:

  1. A favor, to be named later. Not a threat—a genuine IOU. Lew trades in favors the way other people trade in iron. "One day, I’ll need something found. Or someone kept unfound. And you’ll help. Fair?"

  2. Help with a local problem before he leaves. Lew can’t just walk out of Stairwatch. He has obligations, debts, and a reputation to maintain. He needs the PCs to handle something first.

Part 4: Lew’s Local Task

Lew has a problem he can’t solve himself because solving it would burn his cover as "just a finder."

Choose one (or both) of the following tasks based on session time and party preferences:

Option A: The Missing Miners

Three miners went into the deep shafts four days ago and haven’t returned. The Mine Head foreman sealed the tunnel and declared "bad air." Lew doesn’t believe it—he thinks something took them. He wants the PCs to go into the mine and bring them back (or bring back proof of what happened).

What’s Down There:

  • The deep shafts broke into an old barrow-complex—pre-Hrafnborg, pre-Svellheim, something older. The miners broke through a wall and found a chamber with runic carvings and a cold that isn’t natural.

  • Two miners are alive, trapped behind a cave-in. One miner is dead—killed by a Barrow-Wight (use Draugr Barrow-Warden stats from Beat 2, or scale up) that guards the old chamber.

  • The runic carvings (medium Reason test) reference "the binding below" and "the wolf that waits." This is a fragment of Grafvitnir lore—the PCs won’t understand it yet, but it connects to the Act 1 climax.

Encounter: 1 Barrow-Wight (Level 2 Boss Standard) + environmental hazards (unstable ceiling, flooded passages, choking dust).

Option B: The Debt Collector

A man named Rath has arrived in Stairwatch claiming Lew owes him a significant debt from a previous contract. Rath is technically correct—Lew took payment for a finding-job and then didn’t deliver, because the thing he was hired to find turned out to be a person who was hiding for good reason.

Lew needs the PCs to either:

  • Negotiate with Rath (Moderate Negotiation—Interest 4, Patience 3, Motivations: Justice, Respect; Pitfalls: Lies, Evasion) — convince him to accept alternative payment or forgive the debt.

  • Investigate Rath’s purpose — Rath isn’t just a debt collector. He’s working for someone who wants that hidden person found. If the PCs dig (medium Intuition test, or social investigation), they learn Rath’s employer has connections to the Pale Maw. This task becomes a moral choice: pay the debt (funding enemies) or expose Rath (burning Lew’s reputation).

⚔️ Alternate Reward

If the PCs help Lew with the Debt Collector task, Lew produces a weapon from his personal stash as partial payment: the @UUID[Item.SVH00ChainDagr01]{Chainwarden’s Confiscated Dagger}. “Took it off a Warden who owed me a favour. The favour is yours now.” (Only available if the PCs did not already receive it from Eirik in Beat 4.)

Part 5: The Eye

After the PCs complete Lew’s task, he’s satisfied they’re competent and trustworthy enough to travel with. Over a drink at the Broken Stair (or around a campfire if they completed the mine task), Lew explains how he’ll find Kaelen.

Lew reaches up and taps the amber eye—the one that catches the light wrong. He doesn’t remove it, but he turns his head so you can see it clearly.

"Lost the real one years ago. Bad job, bad luck, bad timing—the usual story when something bites you that shouldn’t exist." He shrugs. "Gragnir pulled me out. Stitched me up. And when I was healing, he gave me this."

He taps it again. The amber gleams.

"It’s a gem. Old. He said it was payment for the job—fair trade for an eye, he said." A grim smile. "Took me months to realize what it could do. I was looking for a lost shipment of pelts—routine job—and the eye just…​ pulled. Like a compass needle. Pointed me right to the cache, buried under a snowdrift twelve miles from where anyone was looking."

He leans back. "Gragnir didn’t know. Or maybe he did and wanted me to figure it out myself. With him, it’s hard to tell."

📝 GM Notes: The Eye’s Nature

The gem is genuine—a fragment of something old, possibly connected to Yggdrasil’s sap-amber (fossilized world-tree resin). Gragnir gave it as honest payment, not as a tool. The finding-ability was serendipity: a happy accident of a powerful component in the right host.

The eye works like this:

  • When Lew focuses on something lost (a person, an object, a place that was known and became unknown), the eye warms and pulls in the correct direction.

  • It doesn’t give distances or details—just direction. "That way. Far."

  • It doesn’t work on things that were never known (you can’t find something nobody lost).

  • It doesn’t work on things that are hidden by Wyrd-level power (e.g., Grafvitnir’s prison, the deepest roots of Yggdrasil). It can find Kaelen because Kaelen is hiding by choice, not by cosmic force.

The eye establishes:

  • Lew and Gragnir have history. They’ve worked together before. Lew respects Gragnir but finds him infuriating.

  • Gragnir’s gifts are real but not calculated. He helps people in ways that turn out to be important—but he didn’t necessarily plan for it.

  • Lew’s gift is rare but limited. It’s a compass, not a map. The PCs still have to get to Kaelen.

Lew Uses the Eye

When Lew focuses on Kaelen:

Lew closes his good eye. For a moment, nothing happens. Then the amber eye shifts—not the lid, not the socket, but the light inside the gem. It brightens, dims, brightens again, like a heartbeat.

Lew’s hand comes up slowly and points southeast. Through the wall of the inn, through the mountain, toward the vast grey emptiness of the Interior Belt.

"She’s there," he says. "Past the Shield. Past the dead farms. Deep in the Ashen Woods—what’s left of them." His voice is quiet. "She’s alive. And she doesn’t want to be found."

He opens his eye. The amber fades.

"That’s the Great Green, friends. Or what used to be it. Three weeks' travel if we’re lucky. More if we’re not." He pauses. "And we won’t be."

Part 6: Level Up

At this point, the PCs have:

  • Survived the road to Hrafnborg (Beat 2)

  • Received the mission from Harald (Beat 3)

  • Crossed Midgard’s Shield to Stairwatch (Beat 4)

  • Completed Lew’s local task (Beat 5)

  • Secured an ally and a heading

The party advances to Level 2.

📝 GM Notes: Level-Up Moment

This is a natural break point. Let the players take a short rest, discuss their build choices, and settle into the feeling of having survived the first phase of the journey.

Narratively, frame it as: the PCs have proven they can handle the road, the cold, and the unexpected. They’re not wanderers anymore—they’re agents of something larger, even if they don’t fully understand it yet.

Part 7: Departure

Lew needs one day to settle his affairs in Stairwatch (or less, if the PCs are in a hurry and help him pack). He travels light: a pack, a knife, his whittling bone, and equipment that suggests he’s been on the road more than he’s been home.

Before They Leave

  • Hild the innkeeper packs them extra rations (on the house, if the PCs helped with the mine). "Bring that one-eyed bastard back alive. He owes me."

  • Solvi, the young Ember-Keeper, approaches the PCs at the Ever-Ember. She’s heard they’re heading into the Interior Belt. She presses a coal-ember into a small iron box and gives it to the party: the @UUID[Item.SVH0EmberCoal001]{Solvi’s Ember-Coal Box}. “Take this. It’s not much. But… don’t let the fire die. Please.” (This ember can be used once to ignite a campfire that burns warm for 8 hours regardless of weather. Mechanically: automatic success on one fire-related montage test.)

  • A silver-eyed crow perches on the gate post, watching the road southeast. It doesn’t startle when approached — just tilts its head, then lifts away south. (GM note: A Wyrd-touched bird. Grafvitnir’s network uses corrupted carrion birds as spotters. Dreyfus, still at Harald’s side in Hrafnborg, relayed the party’s planned route via Wyrd-channels when he overheard their departure preparations.)

Grafvitnir Pressure Check

Roll the Grafvitnir Pressure Check (d6) as the PCs prepare to leave:

Roll Effect
1-2 No activity. Dreyfus’s intelligence hasn’t reached field agents yet — the network is slow this far from Hrafnborg.
3-4 A stranger at the Bone Market asks Hild about the PCs' destination. Hild says nothing, but mentions it to the PCs as a warning. (GM note: A Pale Maw informant acting on intelligence relayed through the network.)
5 The Chainwarden post receives a raven the morning the PCs plan to leave. Deputy Freja comes to the inn asking pointed questions. The PCs must talk fast or leave immediately. (GM note: A corrupted official responding to network directives.)

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Found and allied with Lew Tosferd.

  • Completed a local task that established their competence and moral compass.

  • Learned that Lew and Gragnir have a shared history (the eye, the old job, the injury).

  • Learned Kaelen’s direction: deep in the Ashen Woods (the Great Green).

  • Advanced to Level 2.

  • Felt Grafvitnir’s network tightening (suspicious strangers, pointed questions, silver-eyed birds, a sense of being watched).

Transition to Beat 6

The party departs Stairwatch heading southeast with Lew as guide. The road ahead passes through failing farmland, abandoned villages, and finally into the skeletal remains of the Great Greenweald—now called the Ashen Woods.

Proceed to Beat 6: The Great Green.

Level: 2
Duration: 2–3 sessions
Location: Interior Belt → Ashen Woods (or Bone Fields / Frozen Steppe) → approaching the Green Heart
Key NPCs: Lew Tosferd (guide), Gragnir (possible brief appearance)
Type: Hard Montage Test (3 rounds), 2 combat encounters, negotiation, route choice, Grafvitnir network pressure
Tone: Haunted landscape, creeping dread, beauty amid ruin, the world is dying

Overview

The longest leg of Act 1. The PCs travel southeast from Stairwatch, across the failing Interior Belt, and ultimately to the Green Heart—the last surviving grove of the Great Greenweald.

Distance: ~160 miles via the Ashen Woods (primary route), or ~125 miles via the Bone Fields and Frozen Steppe (alternate route)
Duration: ~9 days (Ashen Woods) or ~7 days (Bone Fields) — mildly hindered terrain throughout (18 miles/day)
Calendar: Departure approximately Sólmánuður 20th; arrival approximately Sólmánuður 28th–29th

The journey is broken into three montage test rounds separated by combat encounters, narrative interludes, and points of interest. By the time the PCs reach the Green Heart, they should understand what the world has lost—and what, impossibly, still clings to life.

Part 1: The Route Choice

Before departure from Stairwatch, Lew presents the options. This is a meaningful player decision.

Lew spreads a stained map across the table at the Broken Stair. His amber eye glows faintly—brighter when his finger traces southeast.

"Two ways to get where we’re going," he says, tapping the map. "Neither is good."

He draws a long curve south. "Through the Ashen Woods. What’s left of the Great Greenweald. Hundred and sixty miles of charcoal and silence. Slow going—the roads are gone, the ground is treacherous, and the things living in the char don’t like visitors." He pauses. "But there’s cover. Shelter. And the woods are…​ connected to where we’re going. We’ll find the trail."

His finger shifts east, cutting a straighter line across open ground. "Or through the Bone Fields. Hundred and twenty-five miles—shorter, faster. But the Fields are open country. No cover, no shelter, and the cold is worse than anything you’ve felt. There’s a reason they call it the Frozen Steppe on the far side." He taps his amber eye. "I’ve been through the Fields before. With Gragnir. It didn’t go well."

He looks at you.

"Your call."

📝 GM Notes: Route Choice

Both routes lead to the Green Heart. The choice affects the character of the journey, not the destination.

Ashen Woods (160 miles, ~9 days):

  • Longer but with more shelter and cover.

  • The PCs travel through devastated forest—oppressive, haunted, but navigable.

  • Grafvitnir’s network has a harder time tracking them (the dead woods confuse the Wyrd-touched carrion birds and informant chains).

  • More biological threats: blighted beasts, poisoned water, corrupted root-things.

  • Lew’s eye pulls them unerringly through the pathless interior.

  • Better connection to the Green Heart’s story—they see what the world lost.

Bone Fields / Frozen Steppe (125 miles, ~7 days):

  • Shorter but brutally exposed.

  • The Bone Fields are an ancient killing ground—bones of men and giants jut from the frozen earth. The Steppe beyond is flat, featureless tundra.

  • Grafvitnir’s network can observe them easily on open ground (+1 to all Grafvitnir Pressure Checks on this route).

  • More undead threats: the Fields are rich with restless dead. The Steppe’s cold tests endurance.

  • Lew has personal history here (opens his backstory about Gragnir earlier).

  • Greater tactical risk but faster arrival—important if the PCs suspect a time pressure.

If the players can’t decide, Lew recommends the Ashen Woods: "I know the Fields. That’s exactly why I don’t want to go back."

The Ashen Woods route is detailed as the primary path below. The Bone Fields route follows as an alternate path.

The Ashen Woods Route

Part 2: Setting the Scene

The Dead Farms

South of Stairwatch, the world opens up. The mountains fall behind you, and the Interior Belt unfolds: flat grey fields that should be farmland, dotted with crofts that have gone dark. Fences still stand, but nothing grows behind them. Silos stand open and empty. Animal pens hold only snow.

Lew walks ahead, his amber eye half-closed, one hand gesturing southeast like a man following a sound only he can hear.

"She’s still there," he says, every morning and every evening. "Still alive. Still hiding."

The road—what’s left of it—leads into the grey.

The Ashen Woods

The first sign is the smell: charcoal and wet ash, as if the fire that burned here never quite stopped smouldering. Then the trees begin.

They aren’t trees anymore. They’re pillars of charcoal—sixty, seventy feet tall, stripped of bark and branch, standing in rows that go on forever. The Great Greenweald was a forest. Now it’s a graveyard of wood.

Nothing moves. Nothing calls. The only sound is the crunch of char under your boots and the occasional crack of a dead trunk giving way to its own weight.

The sky is grey. The ground is grey. The trees are black.

And deep in the distance, Lew’s eye pulls you on.

📝 GM Notes: The Ashen Woods Atmosphere

The Ashen Woods are not dangerous because of obvious monsters. They’re dangerous because they’re wrong. The War of Fire (Year 2 of Fimbulwinter) burned the Great Greenweald deliberately—to deny cover to enemies and produce fuel. What’s left is a landscape that shouldn’t exist: a forest-shaped absence.

Key sensory details to deploy:

  • Silence. No birds, no insects, no wind through leaves. The silence is aggressive—it pushes back against conversation.

  • Charcoal dust. Everything is covered in a fine black powder. It gets in clothes, lungs, eyes. By the end of day one, the PCs are grey-black from head to foot.

  • Scale. The forest was enormous. The dead trunks go on for miles in every direction. It takes days to cross. The monotony is its own hazard.

  • The occasional green. Every few miles, a small patch of moss or a single green shoot pushes through the char. These are the woods trying to come back. They’re failing, but they’re trying.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes + Lew as NPC companion):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 3 montage test rounds. Insert combat encounters between rounds.

Lew’s Contribution: Lew assists one PC’s test per round (his choice if the players don’t ask). He uses Intuition (+4) or Reason (+2) to assist. He also provides automatic navigation—the PCs cannot get lost while Lew’s eye is active. They can be delayed, ambushed, or worn down, but not lost.

Round 1 Interlude: The Last Farm

Between Round 1 and the combat encounter, stage this brief scene:

The last farmstead before the Ashen Woods is larger than the others. A proper longhouse, stone-built, with a turf roof that’s still mostly intact. The door is barred from the outside.

Inside: a family’s belongings, neatly packed. A note on the table, weighted with a stone. The handwriting is shaky:

"We go southwest. May the Ember at Hrafnborg still burn. If you read this and the fire is out—God help you. God help us all."

The Ever-Ember niche in the wall is cold. The ash is weeks old.

Let the PCs react. If they search the house: preserved food (1 day’s rations), a good hand-axe, and a child’s doll left behind on a bed. The doll’s face is turned toward the south—toward the woods—as if watching something.

Part 4: Combat Encounter — Rot-Beasts at the Treeline

As the PCs leave the farms and enter the Ashen Woods, the first real threats emerge.

Context

The Ashen Woods' charcoal shell hides something worse than dead trees. The burning didn’t kill everything—some things changed. Animals warped by the blight that followed the fire: rot-touched, aggressive, territorial. They don’t attack for food. They attack because their territory has been violated.

The Encounter

Setting: The treeline—the border between open farmland and the burnt forest. Charcoal trunks rise like prison bars. The ground is soft with ash and hidden roots.

Enemies:

  • 2 Rot-Beasts (Level 2 Brute Standard)

  • 1 Vine Strangler (Level 2 Ambusher Standard)

Rot-Beast (Blighted Boar)

Rot-Beast — Draw Steel NPC

Level 2 Brute Standard | EV 10 | Beast, Blighted

Stamina: 40 | Speed: 6 (walk) | Size: M | Stability: 2 | Free Strike: 5

Characteristics: Might +3, Agility +1, Reason −3, Intuition +1, Presence −2

Features:

  • Charge: When the Rot-Beast moves 3+ squares in a straight line before a strike, the strike deals +5 damage.

  • Blighted Hide: Any creature that makes a melee free strike against the Rot-Beast takes 3 corruption damage (thorns, spines, and fungal barbs).

Abilities:

  • Gore (Action, Melee 1, Might): 4/7/10 + Might damage. On tier 3, the target is pushed 2 squares and falls prone.

  • Frenzy (Triggered: the Rot-Beast drops below half stamina): The Rot-Beast gains +2 to all damage and +2 speed for the rest of combat. It attacks the nearest creature, ally or enemy.

Director Notes: Open with a Charge for maximum impact. Rot-Beasts are dumb and aggressive—they don’t retreat, don’t coordinate, and become more dangerous as they die. The Frenzy trigger is their defining moment; signal it dramatically ("The beast screams—a sound like tearing wood—and its eyes blaze green").

Vine Strangler

Vine Strangler — Draw Steel NPC

Level 2 Ambusher Standard | EV 10 | Plant, Blighted

Stamina: 30 | Speed: 4 (walk), 6 (burrow through ash/soil) | Size: M | Stability: 0 | Free Strike: 4

Characteristics: Might +2, Agility +3, Reason −4, Intuition +2, Presence −3

Features:

  • Ambush: The Vine Strangler is hidden at the start of combat (buried in the ash). Its first attack from hiding has an edge and deals +3 damage.

  • Rootwalk: The Vine Strangler can burrow through ash and soil at speed 6. While burrowed, it cannot be targeted by attacks.

Abilities:

  • Constrict (Action, Melee 1, Might): 3/6/9 + Might damage. On tier 2+, the target is grabbed (the target takes a bane on the Escape Grab maneuver). While grabbed, the target takes 3 ongoing damage at the start of each of their turns.

  • Drag Under (Maneuver, Melee 1): A grabbed creature is pulled 1 square toward the Vine Strangler and the Strangler burrows, dragging the target partially underground. The target is restrained instead of grabbed until they escape (same DC). The Vine Strangler is treated as having cover while partially burrowed.

Director Notes: Open from hiding—burst out of the ash to Constrict a squishy target (caster or shadow). Then Drag Under to isolate them. The Rot-Beasts serve as distractions while the Strangler does the real damage. If Lew is targeted, he uses Smoke Bomb to escape—he’s not supposed to die here.

Terrain Features

  • Charcoal Trunks: Provide cover but are brittle. A trunk can be toppled (easy Might test, Action) onto an adjacent creature: 5 damage and the creature is prone. Each trunk can only be toppled once.

  • Ash Drifts: Difficult terrain (3+ feet deep). Moving through them kicks up choking clouds—a creature that ends its turn in an ash drift takes 2 damage (inhaled particulates) unless they cover their mouth.

  • Hidden Roots: In 3 random squares, concealed roots trip creatures. A creature entering the square for the first time must make an easy Agility test or fall prone.

Aftermath

The beasts lie still—or what’s left of them. Up close, they barely look like animals. The boars' tusks are wood. Their hides are patchy with bark and fungus. The vine-thing dissolves into the ash, already rotting.

Lew looks at the remains and says nothing for a long time.

"The woods are dying," he says finally. "Everything in them is dying. But dying things fight hardest."

If the PCs investigate the remains (easy Reason test): the blight is organic, not magical. Something in the soil—in the root system—is corrupt. The trees burned, but the roots didn’t. Whatever lives in those roots is spreading poison through the ground.

Part 5: Montage Test — Round 2: Deep in the Ashen Woods (Days 4–6)

After the combat, the montage continues. The woods close in. The silence deepens.

Round 2 Sample Tests

Resist the silence Presence The Ashen Woods are psychologically oppressive. The silence gets inside your head. Characters who fail this test become rattled (−2 to Presence tests) until the next rest.

Round 2 Interlude: The Green Thread

Midway through the deep woods, Lew stops.

"Shh." Lew holds up a hand. His amber eye is open, bright, almost pulsing.

He kneels and brushes ash away from the ground. Beneath the char—green. A single root, thick as a man’s arm, running through the dead soil. It’s alive. Pale green bark, sap beading at the surface, warm to the touch.

Lew follows it with his fingers. It runs southeast—the same direction the eye has been pulling. Toward the centre of the woods.

"That’s not a tree root," he says quietly. "That’s a taproot. Something at the centre of these woods is still alive. And it’s been feeding the whole forest from below, trying to bring it back."

He looks up at you. "That’s where she is."

This root is a tendril of the Green Heart—the surviving grove. It is old, deep, and connected ultimately to Yggdrasil’s root network (though the PCs won’t know this yet). Kaelen lives at the centre of this network.

Part 6: Point of Interest — The Charcoal Cathedral

Place this between Round 2 and Round 3, as the PCs push deeper into the Ashen Woods.

The trees change. Not in colour—still black, still dead—but in arrangement. The trunks here grow closer together, and their upper branches interlock overhead, forming a natural vault. The charcoal ceiling is fifty feet up. Ash motes drift downward like black snow.

It looks like a cathedral. A vast nave of dead wood, pillared and vaulted, stretching southeast into darkness.

And on the "altar"—a flat slab of stone in the clearing at the cathedral’s centre—someone has left an offering: a bowl of still-warm porridge, a clay cup of water, and a sprig of green holly.

Someone is tending this place. Someone who is alive, nearby, and careful.

If the PCs wait: nothing happens. The offering is left by a charcoal-burner—one of the last humans living in the Ashen Woods. She will not show herself unless the PCs leave an offering in return.

If the PCs leave food, a tool, or a kind word:

You hear a sound you haven’t heard in the woods: a human throat clearing.

A woman emerges from behind a pillar. She is thin—terribly thin—with skin stained grey-black by years of charcoal dust. Her clothes are ash-coloured furs. She carries a handaxe and a leather satchel.

"You’re heading for the green place," she says. It is not a question.

This is Solveig the Burner—a former woodcutter who refused to leave when the forest was put to the torch. She has lived in the Ashen Woods for three years, burning charcoal for fuel, navigating by the root-patterns beneath the ash, and avoiding the blighted beasts.

  • What Solveig knows: She’s seen the Green Heart from a distance—a "wall of thorns and light." She’s also seen the root-system growing beneath the ash. "The ground is alive under the dead. The roots glow at night—amber, like your friend’s eye." She glances at Lew.

  • What Solveig wants: News. She hasn’t spoken to another person in four months. She’ll talk for hours if they let her. She also wants a knife—hers is worn to a nub.

  • What Solveig offers: If treated well, she shares a shortcut through the cathedral that saves half a day’s travel (+1 edge on one test in Round 3). She also warns them about the Rootgnaw Brood: "There are things that chew the green roots. Big as dogs. Bigger. They come from the deep ash, and they’re getting bolder."

📝 GM Notes: Solveig

Solveig is a survivor—not a quest-giver, not a tragic victim, but a stubborn, practical human who decided the Ashen Woods are her home now. She represents the world’s resilience. If the PCs are kind, she becomes a contact in the woods who may reappear in Act 2 (guiding allies through the Ashen Woods). If they ignore her, the offering goes cold, and the cathedral feels lonelier.

If the PCs harm her or take her supplies: she vanishes into the ash. She knows these woods better than anyone alive. They will not find her.

Part 7: Montage Test — Round 3: Approaching the Heart (Days 7–9)

The final push through the Ashen Woods toward the Green Heart. The forest changes: patches of green appear more frequently. The roots beneath the ash glow faintly at night. The air smells of sap and growing things—faint, but real.

Round 3 Sample Tests

Challenge Characteristic Description
Follow the root-network to the Green Heart Intuition The roots are the map now. They branch, split, and sometimes loop back. Lew’s eye helps, but the PCs must read the root-pattern to find the most direct path.
Navigate a char-storm Might / Agility Wind picks up without warning. The dead trunks shed charcoal in great sheets—a black blizzard that blinds, chokes, and disorients. Shelter or endurance, take your pick.
Treat wounds and illness from the ash Reason Days of charcoal dust have taken a toll: coughing, irritated eyes, cracked skin. Proper treatment (herb lore, clean water, bandages) prevents a condition from setting in.

Round 3 Interlude: The Singing Stone

The path opens into a clearing—one of the few spaces in the Ashen Woods where you can see the sky. At the clearing’s centre stands a stone—not a cairn, not a marker, but a single upright boulder of dark granite, roughly cylindrical, taller than a man.

It’s humming.

Not audibly—you feel it in your teeth, your fingertips, the soles of your boots. A vibration rising from the ground, channelled through the stone, and radiating outward. The ash on the ground around the stone has been pushed aside by the vibration, revealing bare earth—and roots. Dozens of roots, all converging on the stone like spokes on a wheel.

Lew presses his palm against the stone. His eye flares bright amber.

"The Tree," he whispers. "This stone is sitting on one of the Tree’s roots. A deep one. I can feel it all the way down. Miles down."

This is a root-node—a point where Yggdrasil’s deep root network surfaces. The stone was placed here by the original Ember-Keepers as a marker. The PCs don’t know this yet, but in Act 2 these nodes become waypoints for their journey to the Emberwell.

If any PC touches the stone: they feel warmth—genuine, deep warmth, like standing beside a great fire. For one moment, the Fimbulwinter cold lifts entirely. Then it returns, and the warmth fades, and the world is grey again.

If Kaelen were here, she would weep. The stone is still connected. The root is still alive.

Part 8: Montage Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes before failure limit)

  • Arrive at the Green Heart’s outer boundary intact and relatively healthy.

  • Earn 2 Victories.

  • The party has supplies, composure, and time to prepare before entering the grove.

  • Grafvitnir’s network is behind them — the dead woods disrupted Wyrd-channel tracking and the field agents lost the trail.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • Arrive at the Green Heart’s boundary, but at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Rations are critically low. The party is hungry, cold, and weakened (−1 to Might tests until fed properly).

    • Morale cost: The Ashen Woods have worn on the party. One PC is rattled (−2 to Presence tests until a respite).

    • Time cost: The journey took longer than expected. Grafvitnir’s agents have caught up — the next Grafvitnir Pressure Check triggers automatically at 4+.

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party reaches the Green Heart, but is exhausted and compromised:

    • All PCs lose 1 Recovery.

    • Grafvitnir’s network is ahead of them — agents of the Pale Maw are waiting near the Green Heart’s boundary.

    • No Victories. Director gains 4 additional Malice for the next combat.

    • Lew is injured (twisted ankle crossing the Char-Gorge; he can walk but not run, −2 speed until healed).

Part 9: Combat Encounter — The Rootgnaw Brood

Near the boundary of the Green Heart, where living roots push up through dead soil and patches of impossible green moss glow in the charcoal twilight, the PCs encounter the Rootgnaw Brood—creatures that feed on the Green Heart’s living root system.

Context

These aren’t natural animals. They’re blight-spawn—things born from the corruption in the Ashen Woods' soil that are drawn to the Green Heart’s life the way moths are drawn to flame. They’re trying to eat their way to the source. The dryads of the Green Heart have been fighting them for years.

The Encounter

Setting: A clearing where living roots have broken through the charcoal crust. Green moss covers the ground. The air smells of sap and rot simultaneously. Three large roots—each as thick as a barrel—pulse with amber light.

  • 1 Rootgnaw Queen (Level 3 Boss Standard)

  • Mandible Crush (Action, Melee 1, Might): 6/9/13 + Might damage. On tier 2+, the target is grabbed (the target takes a bane on the Escape Grab maneuver).

  • Acid Spray (Action, Cone 3, Might): 4/7/10 acid damage. Squares hit become difficult terrain (acid slick) for the rest of the encounter.

  • Burrow Surge (Maneuver): The Queen burrows and moves up to her burrow speed. This movement does not provoke opportunity attacks. She emerges at the end of the movement.

Boss Action:

  • Seismic Tremor (Area, Burst 2 centred on the Queen): All creatures in the burst must succeed on an easy Agility test or fall prone. The ground cracks open, creating difficult terrain.

Director Notes: The Queen fights from below. Open with Burrow Surge to reposition, then Acid Spray to control the battlefield. Use Mandible Crush on grabbed targets while grubs swarm the loose PCs. The Boss Action—Seismic Tremor—should be used when PCs cluster up. If the PCs protect the living roots (preventing grubs from chewing them), the roots pulse brighter and the Green Heart sends aid: at initiative count 20 of round 3, vines erupt from the ground and restrain 2 enemies (the Heart is aware).

Terrain Features

  • Living Roots (3 locations): Each root has 15 stamina. If a grub is adjacent to a root and not engaged, it chews the root for 3 damage per round. If all 3 roots are destroyed, the Green Heart’s defences weaken and Beat 7’s negotiation becomes harder (−1 to Patience). If 2+ roots survive, the PCs earn a nature-blessing: +2 to Presence tests during Kaelen’s negotiation.

  • Green Moss: Provides footing in an otherwise treacherous landscape. Squares with moss are not difficult terrain and provide stability.

  • Charcoal Pillars: As before—cover, brittle, can be toppled.

Aftermath

The Queen’s body twitches once, twice, then goes still. The grubs scatter into the ash. The clearing is quiet—but a different kind of quiet than the dead woods. This silence is waiting.

The three roots pulse brighter. Sap beads on their surfaces like sweat. And deep in the southeast, very faint, you hear something you haven’t heard since Hrafnborg.

Birdsong.

Lew points. His eye is burning bright now, almost uncomfortable to look at. "She’s close. Maybe a day’s travel. Maybe less."

⚔️ Loot

The PCs can harvest chitin plates from the Rootgnaw Queen’s corpse (medium Reason test to identify structurally sound plates, or easy Might test for brute extraction). Lew suggests the idea: “That shell’s tougher than anything you’ll find at a smith. Might want to keep some.”

A PC can assemble the @UUID[Item.SVH00ChitinBkl01]{Rootgnaw Chitin Buckler} during a respite. If the PCs protected 2+ living roots during the combat, the chitin retains stronger Yggdrasil resonance — the Director may grant +1 to the reactive damage at 1st level.

The Green Heart is ahead.

The Bone Fields Route (Alternate)

📝 GM Notes: Running the Alternate Route

If the PCs choose the Bone Fields, use this section instead of Parts 2–8 above. The beat still ends at the Green Heart (Part 9: Rootgnaw Brood continues as normal regardless of route taken), but the character of the journey is completely different.

The Bone Fields route is shorter but harsher: open terrain, more undead, worse cold, and Grafvitnir’s network can observe them easily. It also unlocks Lew’s backstory earlier—he has history here.

The Bone Fields: Setting

East of Stairwatch, the mountains give way to broken flatland—a vast, wind-scoured basin where nothing grows and the wind never stops. The Bone Fields.

The name is literal. Bones jut from the frozen earth: ribs the size of longships, femurs as tall as pine trees, skulls half-buried with eye sockets wide enough to camp in. These are the bones of giants—or something older—fallen in a war so ancient that even the skalds have forgotten which side won.

Between the bones: graves. Thousands of them. Stone-ringed barrows, a day’s march in every direction, their capstones cracked and frost-heaved and—in too many cases—open.

Lew stands at the edge of the Fields and goes very still.

"Last time I was here," he says quietly, "the ground split open and something came out of the earth. Something old." He touches his amber eye. "That’s where this came from."

He takes a breath. "Stay close. Don’t touch the bones. And for the love of all that’s warm, don’t open any barrows."

Bone Fields Montage Test

Difficulty: Hard
Rounds: 2 (shorter route, but harsher conditions)

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 5 1

Round 1: The Bone Fields (Days 1–3)

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate by the bones Reason No roads, no markers—just giant bones. The locals navigated by naming the major skeleton formations. Lew remembers some: "The Keel" (a rib cage), "The Throne" (a pelvis), "Old Three-Eyes" (a skull). Without these landmarks, the party walks in circles.
Endure the steppe wind Might The Bone Fields are flat and exposed. The wind is a physical force—constant, bone-dry, and cold enough to freeze exposed skin in minutes. Characters without face-coverings take ongoing cold damage.

Resist the whispers Presence At night, the wind carries sounds that aren’t wind: voices, scraps of language, names. The dead in the barrows talk in their sleep. Characters who listen too closely become frightened (bane on all tests) until dawn.
Keep Grafvitnir’s scouts at bay Intuition The Bone Fields offer no cover. Grafvitnir’s agent-birds — Valravn with silver eyes — can observe the party continuously. Spotting the observers and adjusting course to stay unpredictable helps.

Round 1 Interlude: Lew’s Story

By the campfire, sheltered in the lee of a giant’s shoulder blade:

Lew stares into the fire. The amber eye reflects the flame strangely—the light bends toward it, as if the eye is drinking warmth.

"Gragnir brought me here three years ago. A job—finding something he’d lost. A seed, he said. A seed the size of my fist, wrapped in leather. He’d left it here 'for safekeeping.'" Lew laughs bitterly. "'Safekeeping.' In the Bone Fields."

He taps his bad eye. "We found the seed. We also found something else. The ground cracked open beneath us—not an earthquake, not a cave-in, something deliberate. Like the earth was opening its mouth. And out of the crack came…​ I don’t know what it was. Big. Old. Made of roots and bone and frozen soil. It looked at Gragnir and spoke."

A long pause.

"Gragnir spoke back. In a language I don’t know—something that made my teeth ache and my eye bleed. The thing listened. Then it went back into the earth, and the crack closed, and Gragnir was holding the seed and my eye was gone."

He touches the amber gem.

"He put this in the socket himself. Said it was 'fair exchange for what the earth took.' I didn’t understand then." He looks at you. "I still don’t. But I can feel the green thread, and I can find what’s lost, and my dead eye sees more than the living one ever did."

This is the fullest telling of Lew’s backstory the PCs will get in Act 1. It establishes:

  • Gragnir’s deep connection to the earth and its inhabitants.

  • The Bone Fields sit atop something ancient and aware—part of Yggdrasil’s root network.

  • Lew’s eye-gem is literally part of the tree. It was given in exchange for a human eye, making Lew a living bridge between the mortal world and the deep roots.

Bone Fields Combat: Barrow Ambush

On the second night in the Fields, the dead come.

Setting: The party is camped near a cluster of barrows. The wind has died—unnaturally still. The moon is a pale smear behind clouds.

Enemies:

  • 3 Barrow-Draugr (Level 2 Standard)

  • 1 Grave-Singer (Level 2 Controller Standard)

Barrow-Draugr

If the PCs focus the Singer first (smart), it uses Raise the Fallen to bring back a draugr as a distraction while it retreats. Fire is the answer to everything in this fight.

Terrain

  • Open Barrows: 3 barrows are open. Draugr within 5 squares regain stamina. PCs can seal barrows (medium Might test) to deny this healing.

Aftermath

The dead lie down. The singer’s voice fades into the wind. The barrows are quiet again—but the silence feels temporary, like the pause between breaths.

Lew kneels beside one of the draugr. Its armour is old—generations old—but the runes embossed on the breastplate are still legible. He traces them with a finger.

"These weren’t warriors," he says. "They were farmers. Look—ploughshare runes, harvest-knots. They died before the giants fell. Before any of this."

He stands. "The Fields go deep. Deeper than bone. Let’s not be here when something deeper decides to wake up."

Round 2: The Frozen Steppe (Days 4–7)

Beyond the Bone Fields, the terrain flattens into featureless tundra—the Frozen Steppe. The party must cross this to reach the Green Heart from the east.

Challenge Characteristic Description
Navigate featureless terrain Reason / Intuition No landmarks, no trees, no features. Navigation is by sun-position (barely visible), wind-direction, and Lew’s eye. A wrong heading loses hours.
Endure extreme cold exposure Might The Steppe is the coldest place the PCs have been. The wind-chill is lethal. Characters must manage layers, rotation of exposed skin, and movement patterns to survive.
Cross a pressure-ice field Agility The Steppe crosses a frozen plain where competing ice plates have buckled upward, creating a jagged maze of ice ridges and crevasses. Navigation through is slow and physically dangerous.
Spot the treeline (or its absence) Intuition The Green Heart should be visible from the Steppe—a dark line on the horizon. But the Heart is hidden by some means. Finding it requires Lew’s eye or a keen-eyed scout who notices where the sky’s colour changes.
Resist isolation madness Presence Seven days of travel with nothing to look at. The Steppe’s emptiness erodes sanity. Characters begin seeing landmarks that aren’t there, hearing voices in the wind, or turning around to walk back north.
Manage a supply crisis Reason Seven days through barren terrain with no foraging opportunities. If supplies weren’t carefully managed from Stairwatch, the party faces genuine starvation. Rationing, hunting (for what?), or desperate measures.

Round 2 Interlude: The Sky Changes

Lew stops. He’s been stopping more often—his eye burns, his head aches, and twice today he’s turned in a slow circle and pointed in a slightly different direction, correcting something only he can feel.

But now he’s not correcting. He’s staring south.

"Look at the sky," he says.

You look. It takes a moment to see it. The sky over the Steppe is grey—the same grey it’s been for a week. But to the south, near the horizon, the grey is…​ different. Darker. Richer. Not storm-clouds—something organic. Something that absorbs light rather than blocking it.

"That’s the canopy," Lew says. His voice is awed. "The trees. The real trees. The Heart is still growing."

He points. "Two days. Maybe less."

The party has spotted the Green Heart’s edge from the east. They still need to navigate to it, but they now have a visual bearing. The Heart appears as a dark smudge on the horizon—the only thing taller than grass-height for fifty miles.

Bone Fields Route: Montage Outcomes

The outcomes are identical to the Ashen Woods route (see Part 8 above), with one modification:

  • On Partial Success or Total Failure, add: Grafvitnir’s network has tracked them across the open ground. The Grafvitnir Pressure Check at the end of this beat triggers at +1 (the Bone Fields offered no concealment).

After the montage concludes, the party approaches the Green Heart from the east and encounters the Rootgnaw Brood (Part 9, above) at the Heart’s boundary regardless of route taken.
