The party can see Hrafnborg from the mountaintop — or they can see its light, at least, flickering on the horizon. But between them and the city: open ground, draugr columns, and the worst of the storm. One last push.

Round 3 Interlude: The View from the Summit

At the summit of Titan’s Stair, the wind drops. Just for a moment—a gap between gusts, a breath of stillness in the blizzard.

And from the summit, looking south toward the coast, you see it.

Hrafnborg.

The Ever-Ember’s light is visible even through the storm—a warm amber glow on the horizon, impossibly bright and impossibly small. Like a candle in a cathedral.

But around it: darkness. Not storm-darkness. Something else. A stain on the land, surrounding the city in every direction. From this distance, it could be shadow. It could be an army.

Kaelen’s ember-mark blazes. She gasps and grabs at her forehead.

"It’s still lit," she says. "The Ember’s still burning." She pauses. "But it’s screaming. Something is pressing against it. Something cold and old and patient."

Lew squints into the distance. "What am I looking at? Is that—are those—"

"Yes," Kaelen says. "The dead."

The wind returns. The summit vanishes in white. The PCs begin the descent.

Part 9: Montage Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes before 3 failures)

  • Return journey is swift and efficient. The party arrives near Hrafnborg with supplies, energy, and time.

  • Earn 2 Victories.

  • The undead column is behind them—they’ve outpaced the dead.

  • Kaelen is shaken but steady. She’s ready to face the Ember.

Partial Success (failure limit hit, but 2+ more successes than failures)

  • The party reaches the outskirts of Hrafnborg, but at cost. Choose one:

    • Heat cost: Rations are gone. The party is starving by the time they sight Hrafnborg.

    • Time cost: The return took longer than expected. The undead column is ahead of them, and Hrafnborg is already under pressure when they arrive.

    • Injury cost: One PC was injured in the mountain crossing—a fall, a rockslide, a frostbiten limb. They arrive with 1 fewer Recovery.

  • Earn 1 Victory.

Total Failure

  • The party arrives but is exhausted and compromised.

    • All PCs and NPCs lose 1 Recovery.

    • The undead army has already reached Hrafnborg. The siege has begun and the PCs are outside the walls.

    • No Victories earned. Director gains 4 Malice for the Act 1 climax.

Part 10: Gragnir’s Appearance — The Reveal

This is the turning point of Beat 8 and the setup for Act 1’s climax.

When It Happens

After the montage test concludes, as the party camps within a day’s travel of Hrafnborg — near the base of the mountains on the coastal side. The fire burns low. The night is cold. The PCs can see the distant glow of Hrafnborg’s Ever-Ember on the horizon—or perhaps the glow of fires that aren’t Ever-Embers at all.

The Arrival

The fire pops. A log shifts.

And Gragnir is there.

He’s sitting on the other side of the fire, as if he’s been there for hours. His soot-stained hands rest on his knees. His grey furs are dusted with ash. He looks tired—not the fatigue of travel, but the exhaustion of someone who has been fighting a war no one else can see.

"You found her," he says, looking at Kaelen. His voice is rough. "Good."

Kaelen stiffens. "Gragnir."

"Kaelen."

They look at each other across the fire. Something old and uncomfortable passes between them.

"We need to talk," Gragnir says. "All of us. Now. Before tomorrow."

Gragnir’s Warning

Gragnir speaks plainly for the first time. He is done with riddles.

"Your King has a companion," Gragnir says quietly. He stares into the fire. "A grey hound. Sits at his feet during every council. Sleeps at the foot of his bed. Follows him to the Ever-Ember chamber. Never barks. Never eats. Just watches."

His black fingers tighten on his knees.

"It is not a dog. It is not a wolf. It is a shape-walker — a fragment given form. A spy. And it has been listening to every word spoken in the King’s presence since before you arrived in Hrafnborg."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"It is listening right now. Over two hundred miles away. Sitting beside your King in the dark."

Let the PCs react. The crucial horror: Dreyfus is not here. He is not at the edge of the firelight. He is not on the road behind them. He is in Hrafnborg, at Harald’s side, and has been the entire time. Every conversation the King has had since they left — about the mission, about troop movements, about the city’s defences — Dreyfus has heard. And through the Wyrd-channels — root-whispers carried through Yggdrasil’s dying network — that intelligence flows directly to Grafvitnir’s agents in the field.

The PCs cannot confront Dreyfus. They cannot warn Harald. They are over two hundred miles from Hrafnborg, and the spy is still whispering.

The Truth About Grafvitnir

"Its master’s name is Grafvitnir," Gragnir says. The word falls like a stone. "The Grave-Wolf. An entity older than Svellheim, older than the Ember-Keepers, older than anything that walks above ground."

He stands. The fire dips as if his shadow weighs more than it should.

"Grafvitnir was imprisoned during the First Kindling—when the original Ember-Keepers sealed the root-chamber beneath Yggdrasil’s deepest root. It was not killed. It cannot be killed. It was bound—by fire, by oath, by the roots of the World Tree itself."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"Every Ever-Ember that fails is a lock that opens. Every Keeper who dies is a chain that breaks. When the last Ember goes dark—Grafvitnir walks free. And when the Grave-Wolf walks, the dead do not rest. Not in their graves. Not anywhere."

The Horror of Distance

There is no confrontation. There is no shape-shifting escape. There is only the dawning understanding that the enemy’s greatest weapon is over two hundred miles behind them, curled at the feet of their King, and they cannot do a single thing about it.

Kaelen’s face goes white. "He heard everything. When Harald told us about the Ever-Ember. When he gave us our mission. Every name, every route, every supply count."

Lew is very still. "That’s how they always knew where we were. Not a tracker on the road. A listener in the throne room."

Gragnir nods.

"It has been reporting since before you left Hrafnborg. Everything you told the King, Grafvitnir knows. Your mission. Your route. Your allies." He looks at Kaelen. "Especially you."

"And when you return," he says, "the hound will still be there. Sitting. Watching. It cannot act directly — guest-right binds even shape-walkers. But it does not need to act. It only needs to listen."

📝 GM Notes: Gragnir After the Reveal

Gragnir doesn’t stay long. He came to deliver this warning and to prepare the PCs for what’s ahead.

The key dramatic tension: the party now KNOWS about Dreyfus, but Dreyfus doesn’t know they know. When they return to Hrafnborg, they must decide how to handle this — warn Harald openly (which alerts Dreyfus), try to act in secret, or simply endure the spy’s presence while they work. This tension carries into Act 2.

If the PCs ask questions:

  • "Why didn’t you tell us sooner?" — "Because you needed to see the world first. The dead farms. The Ashen Woods. The Green Heart. You needed to understand what’s dying before I could explain why it’s dying."

  • "Can we stop Grafvitnir?" — "Not yet. Not with what you are now. But you can slow him. The Ever-Ember at Hrafnborg still burns. If Kaelen can speak to it—truly speak—it may tell us how the bindings can be renewed."

  • "What does Grafvitnir want?" — "Freedom. And then? The end of everything green, everything warm, everything that remembers the light. The Grave-Wolf does not hate life. It simply does not recognize it as valid."

  • "Can we expose the hound?" — "You can try. But guest-right is ancient law. Even Harald — especially Harald — cannot simply destroy a creature under his roof. And the shape-walker is clever. If it feels cornered, it will act. Better to know where the spy sits than to drive it into hiding."

  • "Where have you been?" — "Tending what’s left. The roots are sick, not dead. I have been speaking to them—asking them to hold on a little longer. They’re listening. Barely."

  • "What’s our next move?" — "Get to Hrafnborg. Get Kaelen to the Ever-Ember. And pray the flame still recognizes her voice. As for the hound…​ you’ll have to decide what to do about that yourselves."

Gragnir departs before dawn. He does not say goodbye. He says: "I’ll find you when the roots tell me to."

Kaelen watches him go with an expression that is equal parts respect and resentment.

"That man has never once explained himself before it was almost too late," she says. "Not once."

Part 11: Level Up

After the reveal, with Hrafnborg visible on the horizon and the full weight of the crisis now understood:

The party advances to Level 3.

📝 GM Notes: Level-Up Moment

Frame this as: the PCs have crossed a threshold. They started as local volunteers answering a summons. Now they understand the shape of the crisis—the dying tree, the bound evil, the failing embers. They aren’t travelers anymore. They’re agents of the flame.

Level 3 in Draw Steel means access to more powerful abilities and better survivability. They’ll need both for what’s coming.

Part 12: Approach to Hrafnborg

The final scene of Beat 8: the PCs crest the last ridge before Hrafnborg and see what awaits them.

You see Hrafnborg at dawn. The Ever-Ember’s glow is visible from miles away—a small orange star in a grey world.

But around it: darkness.

The walls are intact. The gates are closed. Fires burn along the ramparts—not warmth, but signal-fires. And beyond the walls, in a ring that stretches from the coast to the foothills, the dead stand.

Thousands of them. Draugr, ghosts, barrow-wights, frost-walkers—a silent army of the restless dead, shoulder to shoulder, unmoving. Not attacking. Not retreating.

Waiting.

Hrafnborg is under siege.

Outcomes

By the end of this beat, the PCs should have:

  • Crossed over two hundred miles of deteriorating terrain and bonded with Kaelen and Lew.

  • Witnessed the world’s accelerating collapse: empty villages, draugr columns, failing infrastructure.

  • Negotiated with survivors at Greyfen and seen how desperate the common folk have become.

  • Learned the truth about Dreyfus (shape-walker spy still at Harald’s side) and his master (Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf).

  • Understood the cosmic stakes: every failing Ever-Ember weakens Grafvitnir’s prison.

  • Learned the spy remains at the King’s side in Hrafnborg — a ticking bomb they must decide how to handle.

  • Advanced to Level 3.

  • Seen Hrafnborg under siege—the Act 1 climax is here.

Transition to Beat 9

Hrafnborg is surrounded. The dead don’t attack—they wait. But the city is cut off, supplies are dwindling, and the Ever-Ember burns lower every day.

The PCs must break through the siege line and reach the city. Inside, they’ll learn the final truth: Yggdrasil is dying, and only a journey to the Emberwell can renew the spark.

Proceed to Beat 9: The Siege of Hrafnborg.

Level: 3
Duration: 2-3 sessions
Location: Hrafnborg outskirts, the siege line, Hrafnborg interior, the Ever-Ember chamber
Key NPCs: High King Harald, Kaelen, Lew, Gragnir (brief appearance)
Type: Hard Montage Test, reunion scene, ritual narrative, Boss combat encounter, Act 2 hook
Tone: Desperation, siege horror, revelation, sacrifice, and the pivot from survival to quest

Overview

The Act 1 climax. Hrafnborg is encircled by thousands of silent undead — not attacking, not retreating, simply waiting. The Ever-Ember burns lower every hour. The city is cut off, supplies are dwindling, and morale is crumbling.

The PCs must:

  1. Break through the siege line to reach the city (Hard Montage Test with combat complications).

  2. Reunite with Harald and witness the besieged city’s desperation.

  3. Bring Kaelen to the Ever-Ember and witness her attempt to speak to the flame.

  4. Defend the ritual when the undead sense what Kaelen is doing and breach the walls (Boss combat).

  5. Learn the final truth: Yggdrasil itself is dying. The Embers cannot be sustained — only renewed at the source. The Emberwell awaits.

This beat ends Act 1 and pivots the campaign from find the Keeper to save the World-Tree.

Part 1: The Silent Ring

The PCs stand on the last ridge before Hrafnborg. Beat 8 ended with the image of the siege. Now they must reckon with it up close.

From the ridge, the scale of it hits you.

The dead surround Hrafnborg in a ring three bodies deep. Draugr stand shoulder to shoulder, frost-blue and motionless, their burial shrouds barely stirring. Behind them, taller shapes — barrow-wights in rusted hauberks, spectral figures that flicker at the edge of sight. And farther back, in the fog off the fjord, darker things move — vast and slow.

None of them make a sound.

The city’s walls are intact. Signal-fires burn along the ramparts. You can see guards — living guards — pacing the wall-walk, their torch-light catching on spear-tips. The Ever-Ember’s glow is visible above the roofline: a faint orange star, dimmer than it should be.

No one is getting in. No one is getting out.

Reconnaissance

Before attempting the breach, the PCs can observe the siege line. Allow Investigation and tests:

  • Easy Intuition test: The undead are densest at the main gates and the harbour approach. The southwestern stretch — near the old tanneries — is thinner. The ground there is marshy and unstable, which the dead seem to avoid.

  • Medium Reason test: The siege isn’t tactical in the normal sense. There are no siege engines, no trenches, no supply lines. This is a spiritual blockade — the dead are drawn to the Ever-Ember like moths to a candle, pressing against its warmth. They aren’t commanded. They’re compelled.

  • Easy Might test: A group this size means the barrow-fields for fifty miles around have emptied. This isn’t a local uprising — this is every grave in the Coastal Belt.

📝 GM Notes: The Nature of the Siege

The dead are not under Grafvitnir’s direct command here. The Grave-Wolf is still imprisoned — but as the Ever-Embers weaken, the boundary between life and death thins. The dead simply rise and are drawn to the largest remaining source of warmth.

This is important thematically: the siege isn’t an army with a general. It’s a symptom. A fever. The dead are the immune response of a dying world.

Some GM options for flavour:

  • The dead occasionally shift, like a crowd swaying. No individual moves — the whole ring breathes.

  • If anyone shouts, the nearest draugr turn their heads. Slowly. Then turn back.

  • A few of the dead are recent. Fresh faces. Villagers who died in the last week.

Kaelen’s Reaction

Kaelen stares at the siege line. Her ember-mark flickers — the scar on her forehead glowing, then dimming, in rhythm with the distant Ever-Ember.

"It’s calling them," Kaelen whispers. Her hand goes to her forehead, pressing the ember-mark. "The flame is so weak it’s pulling instead of pushing. It’s trying to feed, and all it can find is… the dead."

She looks at you. Her eyes are wet, and her voice is steel.

"We have to get in there. Now."

Lew’s Assessment

Lew studies the line with his amber eye, which pulses faintly.

"The southwest, near the tannery ditches. The ground’s soft — even dead legs don’t like mud. The line’s two deep there instead of three, and the wights are clustered near the gates, not the margins."

He pauses. "It’s not a door. But it’s the closest thing to one."

Part 2: Breaking the Line (Hard Montage Test)

The party must cross the siege ring and reach Hrafnborg’s walls. The southwestern approach through the tannery marshes is the best route — but "best" is relative.

Difficulty: Hard
Party Size Adjustment (4 heroes + 2 NPC companions):

Difficulty Success Limit Failure Limit
Hard (adjusted) 6 2

Rounds: 2 montage test rounds. A combat complication triggers between rounds.

NPC Contributions:

  • Lew: Assists one PC’s test per round (Intuition +4). His eye picks out the gaps in the line, the soft ground, the moments when the dead shift and create openings.

  • Kaelen: Assists one PC’s test per round (Might +3 or Presence +2). She can suppress her ember-mark’s glow temporarily, or physically shield someone from a grasping hand.

Round 1: The Approach

The party descends from the ridge, crosses open ground, and enters the tannery marshes. They must reach the edge of the siege ring without drawing the full host’s attention.

Test Characteristic Description
Navigate the marsh Intuition Pick a path through frozen mud and tannery runoff without getting stuck or making noise.
Control your fear Presence The dead are right there. Suppress panic and keep moving quietly.
Move unseen Agility Slip between the bodies. Time your steps to the ring’s slow breathing.
Physical endurance Might Crawl through freezing muck, lift stuck companions, push through reeds.
Read the pattern Reason The dead shift in waves. Predict the next gap and signal the group.
Suppress the mark Presence (Kaelen only) Kaelen’s ember-mark is drawing attention. Help her muffle the glow with mud, cloth, or will.

Narrative Interlude: Among the Dead

Between rounds, describe the horror of being inside the siege ring.

You’re among them now. The smell hits first — not rot, but cold. A deep, mineral cold, like the inside of a cave that has never seen sunlight.

They stand around you. Close enough to touch. A draugr in a farmer’s clothes, his jaw hanging loose, frost threading through his beard. A woman in a burial shift, her eyes blank white, her hands curled around nothing. A child.

None of them look at you.

Kaelen has her hand pressed over her forehead so hard her knuckles are white. The ember-mark pulses beneath her fingers like a second heartbeat.

"Keep. Moving," Lew hisses.

Combat Complication: The Watchers

Between Round 1 and Round 2, something notices the party.

A cluster of Frost-Ghouls — faster, hungrier dead that have been circling the siege ring’s edge like sharks — detect the party’s warmth. They don’t raise an alarm (the dead don’t communicate that way), but they attack.

Combat: The Watchers in the Marsh

Difficulty: Moderate (Level 3 PCs)

Special Constraint: This fight must be fast and quiet. If it drags past 3 rounds, the siege ring begins to close — see "Noise Penalty" below.

Enemies:

  • 4 Frost-Ghouls (Level 2 Standard)

Terrain:

  • Frozen marsh (difficult terrain throughout)

  • Tannery channels (1-square-wide trenches, 4 feet deep, filled with icy chemical runoff — creature that falls in takes 3 cold damage and is restrained until they spend a move action to climb out)

  • Dead standing (the surrounding draugr are effectively walls/obstacles — they don’t fight, but they block movement. Treat as blocking terrain.)

Lighting: None. The PCs cannot use torches or lanterns without drawing the entire host. If a PC has darkvision or another light source (Kaelen’s ember-mark gives dim light within 1 square), they fight normally. Otherwise, attacks have a bane.

Frost-Ghoul (Level 2 Standard)

Undead, Medium

Stamina: 24 | Speed: 7 (unusually fast) | Size: M | Stability: 0

Characteristics: Might +2, Agility +4, Reason −2, Intuition +3, Presence −1

Attacks:

  • Frost-Rake (Melee, Agility): +5 vs. Agility; 5 cold damage and target is slowed (save ends).

  • Pounce (Move + Melee, Agility, 3 Malice): The frost-ghoul shifts up to its speed and makes a Frost-Rake attack. If the target is already slowed, they are instead restrained (save ends).

Features:

  • Heat-Seeker: The frost-ghoul has an edge on attacks against targets that are the warmest creature within 3 squares (effectively: anyone holding a torch, standing near Kaelen’s ember-mark, or who has used a fire-based ability this encounter).

  • Fragile Dead: When the frost-ghoul takes 10+ damage from a single attack, it is dazed until the end of its next turn.

  • Unfeeling: Immune to fear, charm, and pain-based effects.

Appearance: Lean, hunched, with elongated fingers ending in frost-blackened claws. Moves on all fours. Eyes glow pale blue. Constantly exhales plumes of freezing vapor.

⚠ Noise Penalty

If the fight lasts more than 3 rounds, the commotion draws the siege ring’s attention. At the start of round 4 and each subsequent round:

  • 2 Draugr Thralls (Level 1 Minions, as per MODULE.adoc) shamble toward the fighting.

  • The available movement space shrinks by 1 square on each edge as the dead close in.

This is not winnable as a sustained fight. The PCs must finish the ghouls and move. If they are still fighting at round 6, the entire host turns — run the retreat rules from the Draw Steel chase mechanics (or simply narrate a desperate scramble with consequences).

Round 2: The Final Sprint

After the ghoul fight, the party is close to the walls but exposed. The siege ring has shifted — the gap is closing.

Test Characteristic Description
Sprint through the gap Might Raw speed and endurance through freezing mud while the dead close in.
Find the postern Reason Hrafnborg’s walls have a tannery waste-gate. Find it in the dark.
Signal the wall Presence Get the attention of the guards on the wall without alerting the dead. Use the King’s cipher, a lantern code, or pure desperate shouting.
Cover the retreat Agility Stay behind, draw attention away from the group, then slip through at the last moment.
Shield Kaelen Might or Presence The ember-mark is flaring. Put yourself between Kaelen and the reaching hands.
Keep Lew moving Intuition Lew is flagging (he’s no warrior). Keep him on his feet and moving forward.

Montage Outcomes

Total Success (5+ successes, 0 failures): The party reaches the postern gate undetected. Guards open it immediately — they recognize the King’s cipher. The group enters without combat. +1 Victory.

Partial Success (reached success limit with 1 failure): The party reaches the gate, but the commotion draws a secondary wave. They make it through, but one PC takes 5 damage from grasping hands, and the guards have to seal the gate with fire and oil. Hrafnborg’s defenses are weakened — −1 to the city’s Defence Track (see Part 5).

Failure (reached failure limit before success): The postern route collapses. The dead surge. The party fights a running battle to the main gate, where Harald orders a sortie to pull them in. Each PC takes 8 damage. The sortie costs lives — 6 Hrafnborg guards die. Harald’s anger and grief set the tone for the reunion scene. −2 to the city’s Defence Track.

Part 3: Inside the Walls

First Impressions

Hrafnborg under siege is a different city from the one the PCs left.

The city you remember is gone.

The streets are half-empty. The market square has been converted into a triage station — rows of cots under canvas, tended by exhausted healers. The Chainwardens' ledger-house has a queue stretching around the block: ration distribution.

Children don’t play. Dogs don’t bark. The only constant sound is the wind and the low, persistent hum of the Ever-Ember — a sound you can feel in your teeth more than hear.

Guards walk the walls in double shifts. Their faces are hollow. Several wear bandages. One is missing an arm.

And over everything, the Ever-Ember burns — small, orange, and tired. It used to light the whole city. Now its glow barely reaches the second ring of buildings.

Details of the Siege

If the PCs investigate or ask questions:

  • Duration: The siege began four days ago. The dead appeared overnight — no warning, no scouts, just a ring of corpses at dawn.

  • Attacks: Three probing assaults so far. The dead push against the walls in waves, then retreat. Each wave is slightly larger. The guards have held, but at cost.

  • Supplies: Two weeks of rationed food. Water is fine (wells are inside the walls). Fuel is the crisis — the Ever-Ember consumes fuel faster as it weakens, and the supply caravans are cut off.

  • Morale: Terrible. Rumours range from "the gods have abandoned us" to "the King caused this" to "the dead want someone specific." That last one is uncomfortably close to the truth.

  • The Pale Maw: Active inside the city. Preaching that resistance is futile, that the world should be allowed to end cleanly. Harald has arrested two of their agitators, which only makes them louder.

Hrafnborg Defence Track

Use a simple Defence Track to represent the city’s ability to hold. Start at 10. Reduce by the montage failure penalty (if any). Further reductions happen during the wall breach in Part 5.

Track Value City Status
10-8 Holding. Guards are tired but disciplined. Walls intact.
7-5 Strained. Sections of wall require reinforcement. Rationing tightened.
4-2 Critical. Breaches patched with rubble. Fires on the wall-walk. Civilian casualties.
1-0 Falling. The dead are inside the second ring. Evacuation to the Ember chamber.

This track is a GM tool — the PCs don’t see the number, but they see the consequences.

Reunion with Harald

Harald meets the PCs in person — not in his sitting room this time, but in the wall-walk command post, a fortified tower overlooking the main gate.

Harald is armoured now. Not the simple wool tunic of your first meeting — iron mail, scarred leather, a sword at his hip that has clearly seen use. His face is gaunt. His eyes are harder.

When he sees you, something cracks behind those eyes. Relief, quickly mastered.

"You made it." His gaze moves to Kaelen. He studies her for a long moment — the ember-mark on her forehead, barely visible beneath road-grime and ash.

"Keeper," he says. Not a question. An acknowledgment.

Kaelen meets his eyes. "Highness."

The silence holds for three heartbeats.

"Then there’s still a chance," Harald says. And for the first time, his voice shakes.

Harald’s briefing (summarise or roleplay as needed):

  • The siege is tightening. He expects a major assault within two days.

  • The Ever-Ember is failing faster. The Keepers' absence has accelerated the decline — the flame has no one to speak to it, tend it, or calm it.

  • He has been unable to reach any other settlements. Hrafnborg is alone.

  • He asks Kaelen directly: "Can you speak to the flame?"

Kaelen’s answer: "I haven’t spoken to an Ember in twelve years. I don’t know if it will still recognise me. But I have to try."

Lew’s Goodbye (Optional)

If the players have bonded with Lew, give him a departure scene. Lew is not a warrior — he’s a finder, and his part of the bargain is complete. He delivered the party to Kaelen and guided them home.

Lew pulls you aside while Kaelen and Harald talk logistics.

"I’ve done what I said I’d do. Found your Keeper. Walked your road. Nearly died twice — three times if you count the mud." He taps the amber eye. "This thing is going dark. Has been since we left the Green Heart. Whatever’s happening with the tree… it’s pulling everything inward."

He holds out his hand. "You owe me a favour. I intend to collect. But first, you have to survive this."

His grin is thin but real.

"Try not to die. Dead men are terrible at paying debts."

📝 GM Notes: Lew’s Status

Lew can remain in Hrafnborg as a background NPC, or he can depart before the wall breach (slipping out through a route "only I know about"). His role in Act 2 depends on campaign direction:

  • If Lew stays: He becomes an information broker inside the besieged city, feeding the PCs intel during Act 2’s early planning phase.

  • If Lew leaves: He surfaces later as a surprise ally or complication — perhaps he’s found something worse on the road, or perhaps Grafvitnir’s agents have found him.

Either way, the favour remains uncollected. Plant this seed.

Part 4: The Ever-Ember (Ritual Scene)

This is the emotional and narrative core of Beat 9. Kaelen attempts to speak to the Ever-Ember — the first time a Keeper has addressed the flame in over a year.

The Ember Chamber

The Ever-Ember burns in a stone chamber beneath Hrafnborg’s central hall. The room is ancient — older than the city above it, carved from the living rock of the fjord.

The stairway descends into warmth. Not comfortable warmth — desperate warmth. The heat of a dying coal giving everything it has left.

The Ember Chamber is circular, thirty feet across, with a domed ceiling carved with runes so old they’ve smoothed to shapes. At the centre, on a ring of blackened stone, the Ever-Ember burns.

It is small. Smaller than you expected. A flame the size of a man’s fist, dancing on a bed of white ash. Its light is orange, tinged with grey at the edges. The shadows it casts are too long, and they move wrong — stretching toward the flame instead of away from it.

The air tastes of ash and something older. Something like sap, or resin, or the memory of a forest that no longer exists.

Seven stone seats ring the flame. Six are empty. The seventh — cracked, its surface scarred with the same ember-mark that Kaelen bears — waits.

The Ritual

Kaelen approaches the flame-ring. She removes her cloak, her gloves, her boots. Barefoot on the cold stone, she sits in the cracked seventh seat.

Kaelen closes her eyes. The ember-mark on her forehead ignites — not the faint pulse of before, but a steady, painful glow. Her face tightens. Her hands grip the stone armrests until her knuckles whiten.

"I’m here," she whispers. "I left, and I’m sorry, and I’m here."

The Ever-Ember flickers. Then, slowly, it reaches. A tendril of flame stretches from the bed of ash toward Kaelen. Not heat — not light — but connection. The flame touches her mark.

Kaelen screams.

Not in pain. In grief.

📝 GM Notes: Running the Ritual

This scene is narrative, not mechanical. Do not roll dice. Let the players watch. Their characters can be present in the chamber, and they can react, but they cannot help — this is between Kaelen and the flame.

The emotions Kaelen experiences (and transmits through the chamber’s resonance) are:

  1. Loneliness. The flame has been alone. No one has spoken to it. It has been screaming into silence for a year.

  2. Exhaustion. The flame is not dying by choice. It is being drained. Something is pulling its warmth downward, through the roots, into the deep dark.

  3. Memory. Flashes of the World-Tree — Yggdrasil as it once was: alive, green, vast, its roots threading through every stone in Svellheim. And Yggdrasil as it is now: grey, hollow, its sap turned to bitter resin, its roots cracking.

  4. A name. Not spoken — felt. Grafvitnir. The Grave-Wolf, gnawing at the deepest root. Not free yet. But close. So close.

  5. A direction. Downward. Northward. To the place where the first Ember was lit. The Emberwell.

This is the campaign’s pivot. Let it breathe. Let Kaelen cry. Let the players sit with what the flame is showing them.

After the Ritual

Kaelen slumps in the stone seat. The ember-mark dims to its usual faint glow. The Ever-Ember is slightly brighter — not healed, but heard. Speaking to it has given the flame strength enough to last weeks instead of days.

When Kaelen speaks, her voice is raw.

"It’s not dying. It’s being eaten."

She looks at you with red-rimmed eyes.

"Yggdrasil. The World-Tree. The Ever-Embers draw their power from its roots — always have. Sap, warmth, life — it all flows upward from the tree into the flames. But something is consuming the tree from below. Gnawing through the deepest root."

Her voice drops.

"Grafvitnir. The name Gragnir gave us. The Grave-Wolf. It’s real. It’s been imprisoned in the roots since the First Kindling, and every Ember that dies is a lock that opens. When the last Ember goes dark…"

She doesn’t finish the sentence.

"The flame showed me a place. The Emberwell. It’s where the first Ember was lit — where Yggdrasil’s deepest root surfaces. If the tree can be renewed anywhere, it’s there."

She stands. Her legs shake.

"But it’s far. South, beyond the Interior Belt, beyond the White Waste. At the Throat of the World."

Allow the PCs to ask questions. Kaelen can answer:

  • What is the Emberwell? "The First Hearth. Where the World-Tree’s root breaks the surface. Where someone — the stories don’t say who — lit the first flame and the Embers spread outward through the roots to every settlement."

  • Can you heal the tree? "I don’t know. The flame showed me the place. It didn’t show me how. Maybe when we get there, we’ll understand."

  • What about Hrafnborg? "The Ember is stronger now. Not much, but enough. Speaking to it bought time. Maybe a month, if nothing gets worse." She pauses. "But things will get worse."

  • What about Grafvitnir? "It’s not free. Not yet. But it’s aware. It felt me speak to the Ember. It knows someone is listening now." She touches her ember-mark. "And I think it’s angry."

Part 5: The Breach (Boss Combat)

Kaelen’s connection to the Ember did not go unnoticed. The ritual sent a pulse of warmth through Yggdrasil’s roots — a flare in the dark. The dead felt it. Grafvitnir felt it. And now the siege army moves.

The Alarm

This can trigger immediately after the ritual or after a brief rest (GM’s choice based on pacing). Alarm bells ring across Hrafnborg.

The bells start on the western wall. Then the eastern. Then everywhere.

You reach the ramparts and see it: the silent ring has broken. The dead are no longer waiting.

They come in a single, massive wave — not at the gate, but at the southwestern wall. The section weakened by the marshes. The section you came through.

At the front of the tide, something larger. A figure in corroded mail, bearing a banner of frozen bone. A crown of black iron sits on its skull. It walks upright, deliberate, and the dead part around it.

A Wight-Lord. And it’s heading for the breach.

Encounter Setup

Combat: The Wall Breach

Difficulty: Hard (Level 3 PCs, climactic encounter)

Environment: Hrafnborg’s southwestern wall section. The battle takes place on and around the wall-walk, with the dead surging below.

Map Features:

  • Wall-walk (2 squares wide, elevated 15 feet): The main fighting platform. PCs and guards hold this line.

  • Breach point (3 squares wide): A section of wall that has partially collapsed from the marsh erosion. The dead climb through here. The Wight-Lord targets this point.

  • Inner courtyard (below the wall, open ground): If the dead get through, civilians and the wounded are exposed.

  • Signal-fire braziers (3 locations on the wall): Bright light in 4 squares. Can be toppled onto enemies below (Action: easy Might test, deals 8 fire damage in a 2×2 area, one-time use per brazier).

  • Oil trenches (1 square wide, along the base of the wall): Pre-prepared defence. Can be ignited (Action: touch with any fire source, burns for 3 rounds, deals 5 fire damage to any creature entering or starting its turn in the trench).

Allied Forces:

  • 6 Hrafnborg Guards (Level 1 Minions, Stamina 1, Speed 5, Spear +3 vs Might for 3 damage). They hold the flanks. When a guard dies, describe it — these are named people with families.

  • Harald is present but commanding the defence at other wall sections. He does not fight here unless the Defence Track drops to 0.

  • Kaelen is exhausted from the ritual. She can take one action per round (either a melee attack at +4 for 6 damage, or use her ember-mark to create bright light in a 3-square radius, giving the undead a bane on attacks within it). She cannot sustain this past 3 rounds.

Enemies:

  • 1 Wight-Lord (Level 4 Boss)

  • 6 Draugr Thralls (Level 1 Minions, per MODULE.adoc stat block)

  • 2 Oath-Broken Draugr (Level 3 Standard)

Reinforcements: At the start of round 3 and each subsequent round, 2 additional Draugr Thralls climb through the breach. This stops when the Wight-Lord is destroyed (its command aura sustains the assault).

Wight-Lord (Level 4 Boss)

Undead, Medium

Stamina: 80 | Speed: 5 | Size: M | Stability: 2 | Free Strike: 6

Characteristics: Might +4, Agility +1, Reason +2, Intuition +3, Presence +4

Immunities: Fear, charm, poison, cold

Attacks:

  • Fellblade (Melee, Might, Signature): +6 vs. Might; 4/7/10 damage. On tier 3: target takes ongoing 3 cold damage (save ends) and is frightened of the Wight-Lord (save ends).

  • Command the Fallen (Action, 3 Malice, Range 10): The Wight-Lord raises 2 Draugr Thralls in unoccupied squares within range. These thralls act immediately with a single move action.

  • Death-Gaze (Action, Range 5, Presence): 6 vs. Presence; 6 psychic damage. On tier 2: the target is dazed (save ends). On tier 3: the target is instead stunned (save ends).

Triggered Actions:

  • Undying Will (Triggered, 1/encounter): When reduced to 0 Stamina, the Wight-Lord does not fall. Instead, it is reduced to 1 Stamina, gains resist 5 to all damage until the end of its next turn, and makes a free Fellblade attack against the nearest creature. It can be killed normally after this.

Traits:

  • Lord of the Barrow: All allied undead within 5 squares gain +1 to damage rolls and cannot be turned or banished while the Wight-Lord lives.

  • Crown of Black Iron: The Wight-Lord continually emanates supernatural cold. Any creature that ends its turn adjacent to the Wight-Lord takes 2 cold damage.

  • Barrow-Sense: The Wight-Lord can sense living creatures within 10 squares regardless of concealment or invisibility.

Malice Abilities (Director spends Malice):

  • Grave-Command (2 Malice): All Draugr Thralls within 10 squares immediately shift 2 squares and make a free strike.

  • Frost Roar (3 Malice): Each creature within 3 squares must make a medium Might test or be pushed 2 squares and take 4 cold damage. On a tier 1 result, they are also knocked prone. The breach point widens — treat the breach as 1 square wider for the rest of the encounter.

Appearance: A towering figure in corroded mail, its skull crowned with black iron. The banner it carries is a human spine wrapped in frozen sinew. Its eye sockets burn with pale green fire. When it speaks, its voice is the grinding of stone on stone.

Oath-Broken Draugr (Level 3 Standard)

Undead, Medium

Stamina: 40 | Speed: 5 | Size: M | Stability: 1 | Free Strike: 5

Characteristics: Might +3, Agility +2, Reason +1, Intuition +2, Presence +1

Attacks:

  • Oath-Blade (Melee, Might): +5 vs. Might; 7 damage. On tier 3: target takes ongoing 2 cold damage (save ends).

  • Accusing Howl (Action, 5 Malice, Range 5, Presence): +4 vs. Presence; 5 psychic damage and target has a bane on their next power roll. (The draugr screams a name — not yours, but someone you’ve failed.)

Traits:

  • Tactical Dead: The Oath-Broken fights with remembered skill. It flanks, retreats from fire, and targets isolated PCs.

  • Unfeeling: Immune to fear, charm, and pain-based effects.

  • The Dead Remember: When the Oath-Broken drops to 0 Stamina, it whispers one true thing — a fragment of memory from its former life. This has no mechanical effect but can provide lore clues or emotional weight.

Appearance: Better-preserved than thralls. Wears the remains of a hirdman’s armour. Its eyes hold a dull, hateful intelligence.

Combat Narrative Beats

Use these to structure the battle’s emotional arc:

Round 1-2: The Surge. The dead pour through the breach. The wall guards hold the flanks. The PCs face the Oath-Broken vanguard.

The first draugr through the breach is a farmer. You can see the calluses on its hands. It lurches at the nearest guard with dead fingers.

Behind it: soldiers. Real soldiers, once — their armour is wrong, their movements are wrong, but the muscle memory of shield-walls is still there. They come through the breach in formation.

Round 3-4: The Lord Arrives. The Wight-Lord reaches the breach. When it steps onto the wall-walk, the temperature drops. Breath fogs. Weapons frost over.

The Wight-Lord places one mailed hand on the broken wall and pulls itself up. Stone cracks under its grip. The banner of frozen bone plants itself in the rubble, and the dead around it straighten. Their shuffling stops. Their movements become purposeful.

The Wight-Lord opens its jaw and speaks. The voice is old and full of gravel and buried things.

"The Keeper has spoken to the flame. The flame has answered. And the answer is: it is too late."

It draws its blade — a black iron thing, sheeted in ice — and points it at your chest.

"Give us the Keeper. Let the rest end."

Round 5+: Desperation. The battle turns on killing the Wight-Lord. When it uses Undying Will, the dead surge. When it finally falls —

The Wight-Lord falls. Its crown cracks against the stone, splitting into three pieces. The black iron corrodes to dust in seconds.

For a heartbeat, the dead freeze. Then — like puppets with cut strings — they slump. Not all of them. Not even most. But the coordination breaks. The wave falters. Guards shout, surge forward, push the thralls back through the breach.

Somewhere on the wall, someone starts hammering. Rubble goes into the gap. The line holds.

Barely.

Aftermath

After the combat:

  • The breach is sealed with rubble. It will hold — for now.

  • The Hrafnborg Defence Track drops by 1 (or more, if guards died or the breach widened via Frost Roar).

  • The dead outside resume their vigil. They don’t retreat, but the coordinated assault stops with the Wight-Lord’s death.

  • If any named guards died, describe the aftermath. Hrafnborg cannot afford losses.

📝 GM Notes: Consequences and the Defence Track

The current Defence Track value determines the tone of the Act 1 ending:

  • 8+: Hrafnborg is hurting but hopeful. The breach held. The PCs are heroes. Harald speaks of the mission to the Emberwell with cautious optimism.

  • 5-7: The city is wounded. The PCs are respected but feared — "they brought the Keeper, and the dead attacked harder." Harald speaks of the Emberwell as a last option.

  • Below 5: Hrafnborg is on its knees. The PCs see stretchers, mourning, accusation. Harald orders the mission to the Emberwell not as hope but as the only thing left.

Let the track inform the emotional register of Part 6 and 7. The mission is the same regardless — but how the PCs feel about it changes everything.

Part 6: What the Ember Said

After the battle, in a quieter moment, Kaelen and the PCs convene with Harald in the Ember Chamber. The flame is steadier now — Kaelen’s communion stabilised it — but the fundamental truth is clear.

Kaelen’s Report

Kaelen stands before the flame. She is bruised, exhausted, and still trembling from the battle. But her voice is steady.

"The Ever-Embers are not hearth-fires. They never were. They’re leaves. Yggdrasil’s warmth flows upward through the roots, surfaces at the Embers, and spreads outward. Every Ember is a node in a living system — warmth, life, the boundary between the living and the dead. All of it flows from the tree."

She touches the flame with one bare hand. It doesn’t burn her.

"Yggdrasil is dying. Something — Grafvitnir, the Grave-Wolf — has been gnawing at the deepest root since the First Kindling. It was imprisoned there as part of the binding. The prison is the root. And as the root weakens, the Embers fed by it — all of them — are guttering."

She looks at Harald.

"The flame showed me the Emberwell. The place where the first Ember was kindled, where the root surfaces. If the tree can be healed — or the prison reforged — it has to happen there."

"But the flame also showed me Grafvitnir. Not clearly. Just… hunger. An intelligence that has been patient for an age, and whose patience is ending."

She takes a breath.

"If we do nothing, the last Ember dies within the year. Grafvitnir breaks free. And then…"

She doesn’t finish.

Harald’s Decision

Harald’s response depends on the Defence Track and the tone of the session, but the core decision is the same:

Harald is silent for a long time. When he speaks, his voice is that of a king delivering a sentence — not of hope, but of law.

"I cannot lead this mission. Hrafnborg needs a king on the walls. But I can give you what I have: supplies, maps, authority."

He looks at each of you in turn.

"You’ve proven yourselves. You found Kaelen. You broke the siege line. You held the wall. I trust you with what comes next — not because I have faith, but because I have no other option."

He draws a deep breath.

"Go to the Emberwell. Find out what the tree needs. Do what must be done."

His voice cracks, just once.

"And come back."

Gragnir’s Final Words

If the GM wishes, Gragnir appears one last time — at the edge of the Ember Chamber, in the doorway, half in shadow. He does not enter. The Ever-Ember’s light seems to bend around him.

"The flame told her true." Gragnir’s voice is low, rough, and carries the smell of peat-smoke. "The Emberwell is real. The road is south, through the White Waste, to the Throat of the World."

He pauses.

"I cannot walk that road with you. Not all of it. The closer we get to the deep root, the more Grafvitnir will feel me — and I have kept my presence hidden for too long to burn that advantage now."

His gaze moves to Kaelen. Something passes between them — old pain, old debt.

"But I will find you when it matters. I always do."

He turns to leave. Then stops.

"The shape-walker — Dreyfus — is still at Harald’s side. He heard every plan you made before you left, and he has been listening to every word the King has spoken since. When you return, the hound will be there, watching. You must decide what to do about that — but do not act rashly. Guest-right binds even shape-walkers, and a cornered spy is more dangerous than a patient one."

A half-smile, ash-dry.

"Trust is the only currency left that doesn’t freeze."

He is gone. The doorway is empty. The ash-smell lingers.

Part 7: The Road Ahead (Act 2 Hook)

The final scene of Act 1 sets the stage for the next chapter.

Preparations

Give the PCs a short downtime scene in Hrafnborg before departure. They can:

  • Resupply: Harald provides cold-weather gear, rations (10 days), maps of the Interior Belt and the northern routes toward the White Waste, and a sealed letter of authority bearing the Cold Iron Crown’s seal.

  • Gather information: Ask NPCs about the Emberwell, the White Waste, the Throat of the World. Most people have never heard of the Emberwell specifically, but the Throat of the World is known in legend — a volcanic plateau beyond the northern glaciers where "the roots of the world break the ice."

  • Personal scenes: Say goodbye to NPCs, write letters, make promises. If Lew is still in Hrafnborg, he makes a final snide comment and refuses to say anything sentimental.

  • Rest: A respite in actual beds. This may be the last time for a while.

The Departure

You leave Hrafnborg at dawn. The guards open the postern gate — the same one you entered through — and you slip out into the grey.

The dead are still there. But they’ve pulled back from the southwestern marsh. Not far. Just enough. Kaelen’s ember-mark glows faintly, and the nearest draugr flinch.

It won’t last. The mark’s strength will fade as you move further from the Ever-Ember. But for now, it clears a path.

The road north stretches ahead of you. The Interior Belt. The White Waste beyond it. And somewhere, at the end of the world, the Emberwell.

Behind you, Hrafnborg’s Ever-Ember flickers. Ahead, the wind rises.

You walk.

Outcomes

By the end of Beat 9, the PCs should have:

  • Broken through the undead siege line to reach Hrafnborg.

  • Witnessed the city under siege — the human cost of the crisis.

  • Watched Kaelen speak to the Ever-Ember and received the full revelation: Yggdrasil is dying, Grafvitnir is gnawing at the deepest root, the Embers are failing because the tree is failing.

  • Defended the walls against a Wight-Lord assault — the climactic combat of Act 1.

  • Received their new mission: journey to the Emberwell to renew the spark (or discover what must be done).

  • Said goodbye to Harald and (possibly) Lew.

  • Heard Gragnir’s final warning about the road ahead and the shape-walker still at Harald’s side.

  • Set out south toward the White Waste and the Throat of the World.

What Act 1 Has Established

Element Status at Act 1 End
The Crisis Fully understood. Yggdrasil is dying. Grafvitnir gnaws the root. Ever-Embers are symptoms, not the disease.
The Mission Pivoted from "find the Keeper" to "save the World-Tree." Destination: the Emberwell.
The Party Level 3. Bonded through hardship. No longer volunteers — agents of the flame.
Kaelen Returned. Wounded by guilt and communion. The party’s connection to the Embers.
Lew Debt settled (mostly). May or may not be present. Favour still owed.
Gragnir Revealed as ancient guardian. Will not walk the full road. Appears when it matters.
Dreyfus Exposed but still at Harald’s side. Grafvitnir’s agent. The party knows the truth — but Dreyfus doesn’t know they know. A ticking bomb in the throne room.
Harald Holding Hrafnborg. Alone. Trusting the PCs with everything.
Hrafnborg Besieged but stabilised. Kaelen’s communion bought time — not salvation.
The World Getting worse. The dead rise. The cold tightens. The World-Tree screams in silence.

Transition to Act 2

The road north. The White Waste. The Throat of the World.

Dreyfus sits at the King’s feet. Grafvitnir knows everything. The Embers are failing.

But you carry the flame’s last direction. You carry an oath. And you carry each other.

Proceed to Act 2: The White Waste Gate.

Director Notes: How to Hide the True Mechanism

  • Let the world know there is a rite, but keep the steps contradictory.
  • Make every "source" incomplete or biased (temple wants control; old ways want payment; factions want leverage).
  • The Druid never lies, but also never gives the whole shape.

When the chosen PC learns the truth, it should feel like a key fitting a lock that has been in the campaign the whole time.
